compounds.name: BPC-157
compounds.mechanism_summary: Upregulates VEGFR2 (angiogenesis), modulates nitric oxide, interacts with dopamine system. Promotes tendon/ligament fibroblast migration and collagen synthesis.

evidence.detail: 50+ rat/mouse studies showing tendon, ligament, muscle, and GI healing. Zero human RCTs. All human evidence is anecdotal/community-reported.

regulatory.note: Included in 14 peptides expected to return to Category 1 per RFK Feb 2026 announcement — status pending

community_insights.top_positive_use: Tendon/ligament healing — highest volume of positive reports
community_insights.top_negative_report: Anxiety/mood disruption — frequently reported, often unexpected
community_insights.common_mistake: Using for gut healing but not monitoring mood/sleep changes
community_insights.pro_tip: Start low (150mcg), monitor mood daily for first 2 weeks before increasing

compounds.name: Semaglutide
compounds.mechanism_summary: GLP-1 receptor agonist. Mimics incretin hormone to reduce appetite, slow gastric emptying, enhance insulin secretion, and reduce glucagon. Acts on hypothalamic appetite centers.

evidence.detail: Multiple Phase 3 RCTs (STEP, SUSTAIN trials). FDA-approved for T2 diabetes and obesity. Strong efficacy data: 15-17% weight loss in trials.

regulatory.note: FDA-approved as Ozempic (diabetes) and Wegovy (obesity). Compounded versions available but regulatory status contested.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Sustained weight loss with appetite suppression
community_insights.top_negative_report: Hair loss (FAERS signal ROR 2.46), gastroparesis, muscle loss (30% of weight lost is lean mass)
community_insights.common_mistake: Not doing resistance training while on GLP-1 — accelerates muscle loss
community_insights.pro_tip: Titrate slowly (start 0.25mg, increase every 4 weeks). Prioritize protein (1g/lb) and lift weights.

compounds.name: Tirzepatide
compounds.mechanism_summary: Dual GLP-1 and GIP receptor agonist. Combines incretin effects of both pathways for enhanced appetite suppression, improved insulin sensitivity, and glucose-dependent insulin secretion.

evidence.detail: Multiple Phase 3 RCTs (SURMOUNT, SURPASS trials). FDA-approved for T2 diabetes and obesity. 20-26% weight loss in trials — superior to semaglutide.

regulatory.note: FDA-approved as Mounjaro (diabetes) and Zepbound (obesity). Compounded versions widely used for cost savings.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Weight loss superior to semaglutide with reportedly fewer GI side effects
community_insights.top_negative_report: Sulfur burps, severe GI distress when switching from branded to compounded, injection site reactions
community_insights.common_mistake: Switching from branded (Zepbound) to compounded without expecting different side effect profiles
community_insights.pro_tip: If switching sources, treat it like starting a new compound — monitor closely for 7 days.

compounds.name: Retatrutide
compounds.mechanism_summary: Triple agonist: GLP-1, GIP, and glucagon receptors. The glucagon component adds thermogenic fat burning on top of appetite suppression. Most potent weight loss peptide in trials.

evidence.detail: Phase 2 data: 28.7% weight loss at 48 weeks (highest of any obesity drug). Phase 3 trials ongoing. Not yet FDA-approved.

regulatory.note: Phase 3 trials by Eli Lilly. Gray market availability ahead of approval. Expected to return to Category 1 per RFK announcement.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Dramatic weight loss exceeding semaglutide/tirzepatide results
community_insights.top_negative_report: Sleep disruption (~8% of users), emotional blunting, suspected fake/underdosed products
community_insights.common_mistake: Starting at high dose — titrate from 1mg. Product authenticity is a major concern with gray market sources
community_insights.pro_tip: If sleep disruption occurs, it's likely dose-dependent and may resolve at lower doses. Overnight glucose dips triggering cortisol is the suspected mechanism.

compounds.name: TB-500
compounds.mechanism_summary: Promotes cellular migration and angiogenesis. Upregulates actin for cell motility, enabling tissue repair. Systemic distribution — works throughout the body rather than locally.

evidence.detail: Animal studies showing wound healing, cardiac repair, and neurological protection. No human RCTs. Fragile peptide bond — degradation is a significant real-world issue.

regulatory.note: Included in 14 peptides expected to return to Category 1 per RFK Feb 2026 announcement — status pending

community_insights.top_positive_use: Systemic healing when stacked with BPC-157 — the 'Wolverine Protocol'
community_insights.top_negative_report: Tumor growth concern (promotes angiogenesis which could feed dormant tumors), paradoxical joint pain
community_insights.common_mistake: Storing at room temperature — TB-500 has a fragile peptide bond and degrades faster than most peptides. Keep cold.
community_insights.pro_tip: Split weekly dose into 2 injections for more stable blood levels instead of one large loading dose.

compounds.name: CJC-1295
compounds.mechanism_summary: Growth hormone releasing hormone (GHRH) analog. Stimulates pituitary to release GH in pulsatile pattern mimicking natural secretion. DAC version has extended half-life.

evidence.detail: Human pharmacokinetic data exists for the GHRH analog. Limited clinical efficacy data for bodybuilding/anti-aging uses. Well-characterized mechanism.

regulatory.note: Research chemical status. Often sold as CJC-1295 with or without DAC — different pharmacokinetics.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Sleep quality improvement and body recomposition when stacked with Ipamorelin
community_insights.top_negative_report: Water retention/edema, lower back pain (GH-mediated), elevated GH effects worse in older users
community_insights.common_mistake: Taking with food — GH release is blunted by insulin. Take fasted, ideally before bed.
community_insights.pro_tip: The CJC+IPA stack is the most well-characterized GH protocol. Start at 100mcg each before bed.

compounds.name: Ipamorelin
compounds.mechanism_summary: Growth hormone secretagogue — mimics ghrelin to trigger GH release from the pituitary. Selective for GH (doesn't significantly raise cortisol or prolactin like other GHRPs).

evidence.detail: Human PK data and some clinical studies. Cleanest side effect profile of the GHRP family. Well-understood mechanism but limited efficacy data for anti-aging uses.

regulatory.note: Research chemical. Often blended with CJC-1295 in same vial by compounding sources.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Sleep quality — most consistent positive report. Clean GH release without hunger spikes (unlike GHRP-2/6)
community_insights.top_negative_report: Mild — headache, dizziness, transient flushing. Considered the safest GH secretagogue.
community_insights.common_mistake: Expecting dramatic body composition changes from IPA alone — it's subtle and best as part of a stack
community_insights.pro_tip: Commonly blended with CJC-1295 in same vial. If blended, reconstitute as one — don't try to separate.

compounds.name: GHK-Cu
compounds.mechanism_summary: Naturally occurring copper-binding peptide. Stimulates collagen, elastin, and glycosaminoglycan synthesis. Promotes wound healing, skin remodeling, and has anti-inflammatory properties.

evidence.detail: Solid in-vitro and animal data for wound healing and collagen synthesis. Topical use has more evidence than systemic SubQ. Anti-aging claims largely unvalidated beyond skin.

regulatory.note: Available as research chemical (SubQ) and in cosmetic products (topical). No FDA approval for any indication.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Skin quality improvement (topical) and joint pain relief when stacked with BPC-157/TB-500
community_insights.top_negative_report: Skin irritation when combined with retinol/retinoids, overhyped for systemic anti-aging
community_insights.common_mistake: Expecting systemic anti-aging results from topical application — SubQ and topical have very different bioavailability
community_insights.pro_tip: If using topically alongside retinol, alternate days — don't apply both at the same time.

compounds.name: Tesamorelin
compounds.mechanism_summary: GHRH analog — stimulates pulsatile GH and IGF-1 release through the natural GHRH pathway. Specifically targets visceral adipose tissue reduction.

evidence.detail: FDA-approved for HIV-associated lipodystrophy (Egrifta). RCTs demonstrate significant visceral fat reduction. Off-label use for body composition is common but less studied.

regulatory.note: FDA-approved as Egrifta for HIV lipodystrophy. Off-label and compounded versions used for body composition.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Visceral fat reduction — the most targeted fat-loss peptide available
community_insights.top_negative_report: Joint pain (1-10% incidence, dose-dependent, GH-mediated), carpal tunnel syndrome, fluid retention
community_insights.common_mistake: Stacking tesamorelin with CJC-1295 — redundant GHRH pathway stimulation, no added benefit, more side effects
community_insights.pro_tip: Joint pain is dose-dependent. If it appears, reducing dose often resolves it. Don't stack with other GHRH analogs.

compounds.name: Thymosin Alpha-1
compounds.mechanism_summary: Immune modulator derived from thymic protein. Enhances T-cell maturation and function, promotes dendritic cell activation, and modulates cytokine production. Balances immune response rather than simply boosting it.

evidence.detail: Approved in 35+ countries (as Zadaxin) for hepatitis B/C. Clinical trial data exists for immune modulation. Not FDA-approved in the US but has more clinical evidence than most peptides in this list.

regulatory.note: Approved internationally (Zadaxin) but not by FDA. Orphan drug status was explored. Available through compounding pharmacies.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Immune modulation for autoimmune conditions (Hashimoto's thyroiditis, chronic infections)
community_insights.top_negative_report: Generally well-tolerated. Occasional flu-like symptoms in first few doses as immune system activates.
community_insights.common_mistake: Expecting immediate results — immune modulation takes weeks to months to show effects
community_insights.pro_tip: One of the few peptides with actual clinical data across multiple countries. Often pre-dosed at 1.6mg per vial — one vial = one injection.

compounds.name: Semax
compounds.mechanism_summary: Synthetic analog of ACTH(4-10). Modulates BDNF expression, enhances serotonergic and dopaminergic neurotransmission. Neuroprotective and nootropic effects via melanocortin pathway.

evidence.detail: Approved in Russia and Ukraine for stroke recovery and cognitive enhancement. Russian clinical data exists but limited Western peer review. Mechanism is well-characterized.

regulatory.note: Approved in Russia/Ukraine. No FDA approval or trials. Available as research chemical in the West.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Cognitive clarity and focus — often described as 'cleaner' than stimulants
community_insights.top_negative_report: Tolerance development with daily use, nasal irritation, limited Western evidence base
community_insights.common_mistake: Using continuously without breaks — tolerance builds. Cycle 5 days on, 2 days off.
community_insights.pro_tip: Intranasal is the standard route. If reconstituting from powder, use a nasal atomizer. Most people buy pre-mixed sprays.

compounds.name: Selank
compounds.mechanism_summary: Synthetic analog of tuftsin (immune peptide). Anxiolytic effects via GABA modulation without sedation or dependence. Also modulates BDNF and serotonin. Dual immune-modulating and anxiolytic properties.

evidence.detail: Approved in Russia for anxiety and neurasthenia. Clinical data shows anxiolytic effects comparable to benzodiazepines without sedation or dependence. Limited Western validation.

regulatory.note: Approved in Russia for generalized anxiety. No FDA approval or trials. Available as research chemical.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Anxiety reduction without sedation — popular among those avoiding benzodiazepines or SSRIs
community_insights.top_negative_report: Tolerance questions with prolonged use, sedation at higher doses, limited evidence outside Russian studies
community_insights.common_mistake: Treating it like a benzo replacement without understanding the evidence gap between Russian approval standards and FDA standards
community_insights.pro_tip: Often stacked with Semax (Selank for calm, Semax for focus). Same intranasal delivery. Cycle with breaks.

compounds.name: Liraglutide
compounds.mechanism_summary: Glucagon-like peptide 1 receptor agonist.

evidence.detail: FDA-approved. 8 PubMed results found, 1 clinical studies.

regulatory.note: ChEMBL max phase: 0. Hydrated via public APIs. Run MCP hydration for full regulatory detail.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Sustained weight loss and glycemic control — LEADER trial showed 13% CV event reduction
community_insights.top_negative_report: Nausea (40% in trials), injection site reactions, pancreatitis risk
community_insights.common_mistake: Not titrating slowly enough — start at 0.6mg, increase by 0.6mg weekly to target dose
community_insights.pro_tip: Daily injection vs semaglutide's weekly. Consider switching to semaglutide for convenience unless liraglutide is specifically indicated.

compounds.name: PT-141 (Bremelanotide)
compounds.mechanism_summary: Melanocortin receptor 4 agonist.

evidence.detail: 8 PubMed results but limited clinical data. Mostly preclinical/mechanistic.

regulatory.note: ChEMBL max phase: 0. Hydrated via public APIs. Run MCP hydration for full regulatory detail.

community_insights.top_positive_use: FDA-approved for female HSDD — works via central melanocortin pathway, not vascular
community_insights.top_negative_report: Nausea (40%), flushing, headache. Skin darkening with repeated use (melanocortin effect)
community_insights.common_mistake: Using as a daily supplement — it's an as-needed medication, not a routine peptide
community_insights.pro_tip: Inject 45 minutes before desired effect. Nausea is dose-dependent — some users find 500-1000mcg effective with fewer side effects.

compounds.name: Cagrilintide
compounds.mechanism_summary: Long-acting amylin analogue that activates amylin receptors (calcitonin receptor + RAMPs) in the hypothalamus and brainstem. Reduces appetite, slows gastric emptying, suppresses postprandial glucagon, and promotes satiety. Engineered with C20 fatty diacid conjugation for extended half-life (~7 days), enabling once-weekly SubQ dosing. In the REDEFINE 1 Phase 3 trial (N=3417), CagriSema (cagrilintide 2.4 mg + semaglutide 2.4 mg) produced -20.4% body weight reduction vs -3.0% placebo at 68 weeks.

evidence.detail: Phase 3 human clinical trial data available (REDEFINE 1, NCT05567796, published NEJM 2025). 36 registered trials on ClinicalTrials.gov. ChEMBL max phase 3 (CHEMBL4802169). PubChem CID 171397054 confirmed; INN registered.

regulatory.fda_category: Investigational new drug (Novo Nordisk); not yet FDA-approved as of March 2026
regulatory.note: No FDA or EMA approval as of March 2026. Under active Phase 3 review as part of CagriSema combination. Not available for clinical use outside of trials.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Combined with semaglutide (CagriSema) for enhanced weight loss beyond GLP-1 monotherapy; reported to reduce hunger at meal onset more robustly than GLP-1 alone
community_insights.top_negative_report: Nausea and GI side effects remain frequent (79.6% in CagriSema group in REDEFINE 1); injection-site reactions reported; concerns about muscle loss with rapid weight reduction
community_insights.common_mistake: Sourcing from unverified peptide vendors; the compound is a large 37-amino-acid acylated peptide requiring cold-chain storage and sterile reconstitution — improper preparation is a significant risk
community_insights.pro_tip: Titrate slowly (start 0.25 mg/week, escalate over ~16 weeks to 2.4 mg) to minimize GI side effects; always use in combination context as solo data is limited

compounds.name: Eloralintide

administration.frequency: Oral, daily or weekly dosing under investigation

compounds.mechanism_summary: Oral amylin analogue (also known as LY3841136) that activates amylin receptors (calcitonin receptor/RAMP complexes) to suppress appetite, slow gastric emptying, and reduce food intake. Structurally modified compared to native amylin and pramlintide to enable oral bioavailability. Amylin acts on the area postrema and nucleus tractus solitarius to reduce meal size and caloric intake. PubChem CID 65938 molecular formula C20H33N5O9 (MW 487.5 Da) represents a smaller reference compound; the actual eloralintide is a larger modified amylin peptide in Eli Lilly's pipeline.

evidence.detail: Phase 3 trials actively recruiting as of March 2026 (NCT07321886, NCT07282600, NCT07353931, NCT07369011 — total >4,700 planned participants). Phase 1 completed in Chinese participants (NCT06916091). 10 registered trials on ClinicalTrials.gov. Eli Lilly sponsor.

regulatory.fda_category: Investigational new drug (Eli Lilly); Phase 3 as of March 2026
regulatory.note: Not FDA or EMA approved. In Phase 3 trials for obesity, overweight with T2D, obstructive sleep apnea, and osteoarthritis knee pain.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Anticipated as the first oral amylin analogue for obesity — significant convenience advantage over injectable amylin/amylin analogues
community_insights.top_negative_report: No community use data available; compound not yet commercially accessible; limited published efficacy/safety data from Phase 1 only
community_insights.common_mistake: Confusing eloralintide with pramlintide (injectable) or cagrilintide (SubQ weekly); these are distinct compounds with different routes and pharmacokinetics
community_insights.pro_tip: Monitor Phase 3 trial readouts expected 2026–2027; await published dose-finding and GI tolerability data before any research context use

compounds.name: Mazdutide
compounds.mechanism_summary: Dual GLP-1/glucagon receptor co-agonist (also known as IBI362). Activates GLP-1 receptors to enhance insulin secretion, suppress glucagon, slow gastric emptying, and reduce appetite. Simultaneously activates glucagon receptors to increase hepatic glucose output (counterbalanced by GLP-1 effect), stimulate energy expenditure via thermogenesis, promote lipolysis, and drive weight loss beyond GLP-1 monotherapy. Multi-omics data in db/db mice show neuroprotection and improved cognitive function via dual GLP-1/GCGR activation. Network meta-analysis positions mazdutide weight loss at -6.47 kg vs placebo (MD), with favorable tolerability vs retatrutide.

evidence.detail: Phase 3 trials ongoing in China (DREAMS-3, NCT04965506 completed Phase 2, 30 registered trials). ChEMBL max phase 3 (CHEMBL5095358). Published Phase 2 RCT data in T2D. DREAMS-3 (head-to-head vs semaglutide) design published 2025. Network meta-analysis published 2026.

regulatory.fda_category: Investigational new drug (Innovent Biologics); Phase 3 primarily in China as of March 2026
regulatory.note: No FDA or EMA approval as of March 2026. Approved pathway primarily being pursued in China (NMPA). Not available for clinical use in the US or EU outside of trials.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Interest as a next-generation obesity drug combining GLP-1 weight-loss benefits with enhanced energy expenditure from glucagon agonism; potential cognitive benefits in metabolic disease
community_insights.top_negative_report: GI adverse events (nausea, vomiting) are the primary tolerability concern; limited data in non-Chinese populations; supply not commercially available outside trials
community_insights.common_mistake: Assuming mazdutide is equivalent to semaglutide — the glucagon component creates distinct metabolic effects including potential increased heart rate and hepatic glucose output not seen with pure GLP-1 agonists
community_insights.pro_tip: Await DREAMS-3 results for head-to-head semaglutide comparison data; note that Phase 3 hypertension trial (NCT07469800) suggests additional cardiometabolic indications being explored

compounds.name: 5-Amino-1MQ
compounds.mechanism_summary: Selective small-molecule inhibitor of nicotinamide N-methyltransferase (NNMT), an enzyme that methylates nicotinamide using S-adenosylmethionine (SAM). NNMT is upregulated in adipose tissue in obesity and type 2 diabetes. By inhibiting NNMT, 5-amino-1MQ increases NAD+ precursor availability (nicotinamide), elevates NAD+ levels, and shifts adipocyte metabolism toward increased energy expenditure. Preclinical mouse data show reduced fat mass, increased energy expenditure, and improved insulin sensitivity. IUPAC name: 1-methylquinolin-1-ium-5-amine. PubChem CID 950107, MW 159.21, formula C10H11N2+.

evidence.detail: No human clinical trials registered on ClinicalTrials.gov. Evidence limited to in vitro and rodent preclinical studies. ChEMBL ID CHEMBL4116828. No INN assigned. Research-grade compound only; sold as research chemical.

regulatory.fda_category: Research chemical; no IND filed; not an approved drug in any jurisdiction
regulatory.note: Not approved by FDA, EMA, or any other regulatory agency. Sold as a research chemical. Human use carries substantial unknown risk. No GMP manufacturing standards apply.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Reported in biohacker communities as a 'metabolic accelerator' for weight loss and body recomposition, often stacked with NAD+ precursors (NMN, NR)
community_insights.top_negative_report: Lack of human safety data; unknown off-target effects; quality control issues with research-chemical suppliers; some users report GI upset and headache
community_insights.common_mistake: Confusing the quinolinium salt form (CID 950107, positively charged) with neutral forms; incorrect dosing — community doses of 50–100 mg are extrapolated from mouse data without validated human pharmacokinetics
community_insights.pro_tip: If used experimentally, source from reputable suppliers with CoA/HPLC purity verification; consider cycling (e.g., 5 days on / 2 off); monitor liver enzymes given NNMT's role in hepatic metabolism

compounds.name: Adamax
compounds.mechanism_summary: Note: PubChem CID 4485 resolves to nifedipine (a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker), not a peptide called 'Adamax'. The community nootropic compound referred to as 'Adamax' or 'Adamantyl-GHK' appears to be a synthetic construct linking an adamantane moiety to the tripeptide GHK (glycyl-L-histidyl-L-lysine), which has known copper-binding and growth-factor-like activities. GHK-Cu promotes collagen synthesis, activates skin repair pathways, and has reported neuroprotective effects in preclinical models via BDNF and NGF upregulation. The adamantyl modification is purported to improve CNS penetration. No peer-reviewed characterization of the adamantyl-GHK conjugate specifically has been identified.

evidence.detail: No clinical trials registered. No peer-reviewed publications identified for the specific compound 'Adamax' or 'Adamantyl-GHK' as a cognitive enhancer. CID 4485 is a mismatch (nifedipine). Evidence base consists only of anecdotal community reports. GHK tripeptide parent has limited preclinical data.

regulatory.fda_category: Uncharacterized research/community compound; no regulatory status in any jurisdiction
regulatory.note: No regulatory approval anywhere. Identity and purity of commercially sourced 'Adamax' peptides are unverified. Significant risk of receiving a different compound entirely.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Reported by nootropic community for improved working memory, mental clarity, and verbal fluency; theorized to work via enhanced BDNF signaling
community_insights.top_negative_report: No reproducible effects confirmed by multiple users; identity verification of the compound is essentially impossible without mass spectrometry; multiple reports of no effect
community_insights.common_mistake: Assuming that GHK-Cu research translates directly to the adamantyl conjugate — the modification changes pharmacokinetics substantially, and CNS penetration claims are unverified
community_insights.pro_tip: Treat as entirely experimental with red-grade evidence; verify compound identity via CoA before use; do not combine with other CNS-active compounds without medical supervision

compounds.name: Adipotide

administration.frequency: Daily SubQ injections in primate studies (community extrapolation: daily cycles of 1–4 weeks)

compounds.mechanism_summary: Proapoptotic peptide consisting of a targeting domain (CKGGRAKDC) that binds prohibitin on the vascular endothelium of white adipose tissue, linked to a pro-apoptotic domain (KLAKLAK)2. The peptide selectively induces mitochondria-dependent apoptosis in the blood vessels supplying adipose tissue, leading to ischemic death of fat cells and weight loss. Demonstrated profound weight loss (~39% body weight) in obese rhesus monkeys over 4 weeks at 0.5 mg/kg/day SubQ. PubChem CID 12364 resolves to hexanediamide/adipamide — a mismatch with the actual peptide structure. No human clinical trial data available.

evidence.detail: No registered human clinical trials (0 results on ClinicalTrials.gov). Evidence from obese mouse and non-human primate (rhesus monkey) studies published by Kolonin et al. and colleagues. No IND filed. Research-only stage.

regulatory.fda_category: Experimental research peptide; no clinical development pathway established
regulatory.note: No regulatory approval in any jurisdiction. Never entered human clinical trials. Primate data showed significant renal toxicity and weight regain post-treatment. High-risk research compound.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Extreme interest in biohacker/weight-loss community due to dramatic primate weight-loss results; used by some researchers at 0.5 mg/kg/day SubQ in short 2–4 week cycles
community_insights.top_negative_report: Severe renal tubular toxicity observed in primate studies (creatinine elevation, kidney damage); weight regain after cessation; profound dehydration risk from rapid fat loss; no human safety data whatsoever
community_insights.common_mistake: Underestimating kidney toxicity risk — primate studies showed dose-dependent nephrotoxicity that did not fully reverse; attempting longer cycles than studied (>4 weeks) dramatically increases risk
community_insights.pro_tip: If used in research contexts, monitor serum creatinine, BUN, and hydration status daily; limit cycles to ≤4 weeks; ensure renal baseline is normal; co-administer IV fluids in supervised settings

compounds.name: AHK-Cu

administration.frequency: Topical: once to twice daily application

compounds.mechanism_summary: Copper-chelating tripeptide Ala-His-Lys complexed with Cu2+ (AHK-Cu). Stimulates hair follicle dermal papilla cell (DPC) proliferation and reduces DPC apoptosis via elevated Bcl-2/Bax ratio and reduced caspase-3 activation. Stimulates human hair follicle elongation ex vivo at 10^-12 to 10^-9 M concentrations. Also promotes dermal fibroblast proliferation, increases VEGF production, and decreases TGF-beta1 secretion. The copper cofactor is essential for activity, likely mediating effects through copper-dependent enzymes (lysyl oxidase, superoxide dismutase). PubChem CID 125181279 (indazole carboxamide compound) appears to be a CID mismatch — the actual AHK-Cu is a small tripeptide copper complex.

evidence.detail: One key peer-reviewed human-cell ex vivo study published (PMID 17703734, Archives of Pharmacal Research 2007). No registered human clinical trials for AHK-Cu specifically. Evidence primarily in vitro and ex vivo. Used commercially in cosmetic products (INCI name). No IND or clinical drug status.

regulatory.fda_category: Cosmetic ingredient (INCI listed); not classified as a drug; topical use only
regulatory.note: Regulated as a cosmetic ingredient under FDA cosmetic regulations, not as a drug. INCI listed. No drug approval required for topical cosmetic use. Not approved for systemic injection.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Topical application to scalp for hair loss prevention and follicle stimulation; incorporated into serums and formulations targeting miniaturization in androgenetic alopecia
community_insights.top_negative_report: Efficacy is modest and evidence base is thin; skin irritation possible at high concentrations; copper toxicity risk if absorbed systemically in large amounts (theoretical at cosmetic doses)
community_insights.common_mistake: Injecting AHK-Cu SubQ or IV — this is a topical cosmetic ingredient with no safety data for injection; some vendors sell it for injection, which is unsafe and unvalidated
community_insights.pro_tip: Use in formulations with penetration enhancers (e.g., liposomal delivery); combine with minoxidil or other evidence-based hair loss treatments for additive effects; concentration range 1–5 ppm typical in commercial serums

compounds.name: AICAR
compounds.mechanism_summary: See literature: FNDC5/Irisin-dependent renoprotection of resistance training in myocardial infar; The AMPK activator A769662 promotes ferroptosis and suppresses disulfidptosis by

evidence.detail: 8 PubMed results but limited clinical data. Mostly preclinical/mechanistic.

regulatory.note: Hydrated via public APIs. Run MCP hydration for full regulatory detail.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Exercise mimetic — AMPK activator. Increases endurance capacity in animal studies.
community_insights.top_negative_report: Zero human efficacy data for performance. WADA banned substance. Very expensive. Potential cardiac risk.
community_insights.common_mistake: Using as a replacement for exercise — AICAR is a WADA-banned research compound, not a fitness supplement
community_insights.pro_tip: Acadesine (the clinical name) was studied for cardiac surgery, not performance. The 'exercise pill' hype comes from a single 2008 mouse study.

compounds.name: ARA-290

administration.frequency: Once daily (in clinical trials); some protocols daily x 28 days

compounds.mechanism_summary: Erythropoietin (EPO)-derived 11-amino-acid cyclic peptide (also known as cibinetide) that selectively binds the innate repair receptor (IRR), a heterodimer of the EPO receptor and the beta common receptor (betacR/CD131). Unlike EPO itself, ARA-290 does not stimulate erythropoiesis, as it lacks affinity for the homodimeric erythropoietic receptor. Activates tissue-protective signaling cascades (PI3K/Akt, NF-kB suppression, JAK2/STAT3 for cell survival) to reduce neuroinflammation, promote Schwann cell proliferation, prevent neuronal apoptosis, and stimulate nerve fiber regeneration. In the Phase 2b REDEFINE trial (N=64, sarcoidosis neuropathy), 4 mg/day cibinetide significantly increased corneal nerve fiber area vs placebo (p=0.012) and increased intraepidermal regenerating fibers. MW 1257.3 Da (C51H84N16O21). ChEMBL ID CHEMBL3545305, max phase 2.

evidence.detail: 4 registered clinical trials on ClinicalTrials.gov including Phase 2b in sarcoidosis neuropathy (NCT02039687, completed, N=64) and Phase 1/2 in depression/cognition (NCT02070783) and T2D/prediabetes (NCT01933529). Published Phase 2b results in IOVS 2017 (PMID 28475703). ChEMBL max phase 2. INN: cibinetide. Development paused due to drug supply expiration (NCT06626971 terminated).

regulatory.fda_category: Investigational drug (Araim Pharmaceuticals); Phase 2 completed; development status uncertain as of 2026
regulatory.note: No FDA or EMA approval. Development by Araim Pharmaceuticals appears to have stalled (supply expiration issues noted in trial termination). INN 'cibinetide' is assigned. Not commercially available.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Used experimentally for small-fiber neuropathy (diabetic, sarcoidosis-related), chronic pain syndromes, and neuroprotection; anecdotal reports of improved nerve pain and autonomic symptoms
community_insights.top_negative_report: Commercial supply is extremely difficult to source; purity of research-grade material is uncertain; the terminated macular oedema trial raises questions about development viability
community_insights.common_mistake: Confusing ARA-290 with EPO or EPO analogues that stimulate red blood cell production — ARA-290/cibinetide specifically does NOT increase hematocrit, but this is poorly understood by users
community_insights.pro_tip: Dose of 4 mg/day SubQ appears to be the sweet spot from Phase 2b data; 28-day courses showed measurable nerve fiber regeneration; longer courses and maintenance dosing not well-characterized

compounds.name: Argireline
compounds.mechanism_summary: Acetyl hexapeptide-3/8 (Ac-Glu-Glu-Met-Gln-Arg-Arg-NH2); a synthetic hexapeptide that mimics the N-terminus of SNAP-25, a SNARE complex protein required for acetylcholine vesicle fusion at neuromuscular junctions. Competitively inhibits SNAP-25 and SNAP-23, partially blocking neurotransmitter release and reducing facial muscle contraction, thereby decreasing expression wrinkle depth. Often marketed as 'topical botulinum toxin' though mechanism and magnitude of effect differ substantially. PubChem CID 71587772 confirmed; MW ~888 Da (Ac-EEMQRR-NH2). Registered INCI names: Acetyl Hexapeptide-3 and Acetyl Hexapeptide-8. Phase 3 clinical trial in periorbital wrinkles completed (NCT01381484, Mahidol University, N=70).

evidence.detail: 6 registered clinical trials on ClinicalTrials.gov including Phase 3 wrinkle trial (NCT01381484, completed), Phase 1/2 in blepharospasm (NCT00942851, completed; NCT01750346, terminated), and Phase early-1 skin care (NCT03878381). Confirmed synonyms: Argireline, Acetyl Hexapeptide-3, Acetyl Hexapeptide-8, DB11709 (DrugBank ID). Published cosmetic efficacy data available.

regulatory.fda_category: Cosmetic ingredient (INCI listed, FDA cosmetic regulations); not a drug; topical use only
regulatory.note: Regulated as a cosmetic active ingredient, not a drug. No drug approval required for topical cosmetic use. Cannot be marketed with drug claims in the US. INCI listed as Acetyl Hexapeptide-3/8.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Widely used in anti-aging serums and creams at 5–10% concentration for periorbital and forehead wrinkle reduction; appreciated as a non-injection alternative to botulinum toxin
community_insights.top_negative_report: Effects are significantly weaker and more transient than botulinum toxin; results are largely cosmetic and wear off rapidly after discontinuation; some formulations contain low concentrations that are unlikely to be effective
community_insights.common_mistake: Using concentrations below 5% (ineffective); expecting botulinum toxin-equivalent results — argireline's SNAP inhibition is partial and reversible, not causing the prolonged nerve terminal blockade of BTX
community_insights.pro_tip: Use concentrations of 5–10% in leave-on formulations; apply to still face for better penetration; combine with moisturizing ingredients (hyaluronic acid) to maximize skin-smoothing appearance; results are appearance-based, not structural

compounds.name: BPC-157 + TB-500 (blend)

administration.frequency: Once or twice daily (SubQ or IM); 4–12 week cycles typical in community

compounds.mechanism_summary: A community blend of two distinct research peptides: (1) BPC-157 (Body Protection Compound-157): stable 15-amino-acid peptide (GEPPPGKPADDAGLV) derived from human gastric juice. Promotes angiogenesis via upregulation of VEGFR2, activates the NO-cGMP pathway, upregulates growth hormone receptor expression, and modulates the dopaminergic and serotonergic systems. Demonstrated accelerated healing of tendons, ligaments, muscle, bone, and gut in rodent models. PubChem CID 9941957 confirmed; sequence H-GEPPPGKPADDAGLV-OH. (2) TB-500 (Thymosin beta-4 fragment, residues 17–23: LKKTETQ): the active actin-binding fragment of thymosin beta-4 (Tβ4). Full Tβ4 sequesters G-actin in a 1:1 complex, preventing actin polymerization, thereby modulating cell motility, differentiation, and repair. Promotes wound healing, angiogenesis, cardiac protection, hair follicle regeneration, and anti-inflammatory effects. Phase 1 human trial completed (NCT04555850); Phase 2 in acute MI completed (NCT05984134); TB-500 fragment Phase 1/2 recruiting (NCT07487363). In combination, theorized to provide synergistic tissue repair via complementary mechanisms (NO/angiogenesis via BPC-157 + actin remodeling/cell migration via TB-500).

evidence.detail: BPC-157: 2 registered trials (NCT07437547 Phase 2 recruiting for hamstring strain; NCT02637284 Phase 1 completed). Primary evidence from rodent studies. TB-500/thymosin beta-4: 17 registered trials including Phase 2 in acute MI (NCT05984134, N=90) and Phase 1 in healthy volunteers (NCT04555850); TB-500 fragment specifically Phase 1/2 recruiting (NCT07487363). No completed RCT data for the specific BPC-157+TB-500 blend. The combination itself has no dedicated clinical trial.

regulatory.fda_category: Research peptides; neither compound approved by FDA; sold as research chemicals only
regulatory.note: Neither BPC-157 nor TB-500 is FDA-approved. The blend is not a recognized pharmaceutical product. Compounding pharmacies in some jurisdictions have supplied these; FDA has issued warning letters regarding BPC-157 specifically. Not for human use per regulatory status.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Extremely popular in the biohacker/athlete community for accelerating recovery from musculoskeletal injuries (tendon, ligament, muscle tears), post-surgical healing, and gut repair (BPC-157 specifically for leaky gut, IBD-like symptoms)
community_insights.top_negative_report: No human RCT data validating the combination; quality control issues with peptide sources (wrong sequence, degradation, contamination); potential for BPC-157 to upregulate growth factors with unclear oncological implications; FDA warning letters to suppliers
community_insights.common_mistake: Using the blend when only one component is needed — BPC-157 (250–500 mcg/day) is typically used for gut/connective tissue; TB-500 (2.0–5.0 mg twice weekly) for systemic repair and inflammation; blending at fixed ratios may under-dose one component
community_insights.pro_tip: Source from vendors with third-party HPLC and mass spectrometry CoA; store lyophilized peptides at -20°C; reconstitute with bacteriostatic water; inject SubQ near injury site for local effects or abdomen for systemic use; most community protocols run 6–12 week cycles

compounds.name: Bronchogen

administration.frequency: Daily or as directed; Russian protocols typically 10-day courses repeated seasonally

compounds.mechanism_summary: Bronchogen is a Khavinson-developed tetrapeptide (Ala-Glu-Asp-Leu) derived from bronchial tissue. It is hypothesized to act as a tissue-specific bioregulator that penetrates cell nuclei, binds to DNA regulatory regions, and modulates gene expression in bronchial and pulmonary epithelial cells. Proposed actions include upregulating cytoprotective and repair pathways in lung tissue, anti-inflammatory peptide signaling, and telomere protection. The mechanistic basis is drawn almost exclusively from Russian-language preclinical and observational studies conducted by the St. Petersburg Institute of Bioregulation and Gerontology.

evidence.detail: No Western RCTs identified. PubMed search yielded no indexed studies on Bronchogen as a compound. Evidence is limited to Russian-language publications from Khavinson's group, which are largely uncontrolled and not replicated independently. No ClinicalTrials.gov registration found. ChEMBL returned an unrelated hit. PubChem CID 6917754 maps to a different chemical (mabuterol HCl), suggesting the CID assignment does not correspond to the Bronchogen tetrapeptide in Western databases.

regulatory.fda_category: Unapproved peptide; not reviewed by FDA
regulatory.note: Available as a food supplement in Russia and some Eastern European countries. Not regulated as a drug in Western markets. No IND filing or NDA on record.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Anecdotal reports of improved respiratory resilience, reduced frequency of respiratory infections, and lung recovery support post-illness in biohacking communities
community_insights.top_negative_report: Near-total absence of independent safety and efficacy data; community reports are unverifiable
community_insights.common_mistake: Assuming Russian clinical experience translates to Western regulatory or evidence standards; conflating with other peptide bioregulators from the same series
community_insights.pro_tip: If using, community protocols typically follow 10-day on / 3-6 month off cycling consistent with Khavinson institute recommendations; sublingual administration often preferred for bioavailability

compounds.name: Cagrilintide + Semaglutide
compounds.mechanism_summary: CagriSema (CagriSema) is a fixed-ratio co-formulation combining cagrilintide (2.4 mg), a long-acting amylin analogue that is a dual amylin/calcitonin receptor (AMYR/CTR) agonist, with semaglutide (2.4 mg), a GLP-1 receptor agonist. Cagrilintide acts centrally in the hypothalamus and brainstem to induce satiety, slow gastric emptying, suppress prandial glucagon, and reduce food intake via AMYR/CTR pathways. Semaglutide activates GLP-1R to enhance glucose-dependent insulin secretion, further reduce appetite, and promote weight loss. The combination leverages complementary and synergistic mechanisms across central satiety signaling, incretin axis, and energy expenditure, achieving greater weight reduction and glycemic control than either component alone in Phase 3 trials (REDEFINE and REIMAGINE programs).

evidence.detail: Extensive Phase 2 and Phase 3 RCT data available. ChEMBL lists cagrilintide at max phase 3 (CHEMBL4802169) and semaglutide at max phase 4 (CHEMBL2108724). ClinicalTrials.gov shows 28+ registered studies including completed Phase 3 (NCT06065540, n=2,734 T2D; NCT06388187 obesity). Multiple peer-reviewed publications in Endocrine Reviews and npj Metabolic Health confirm Phase 3 efficacy. PubMed search yielded high-quality systematic reviews and original trials.

regulatory.fda_category: Investigational New Drug; Novo Nordisk NDA under development; Phase 3 complete for obesity and T2D indications as of early 2026
regulatory.note: Not yet approved by FDA or EMA as of March 2026. Semaglutide (Ozempic, Wegovy) is approved; cagrilintide as monotherapy and the CagriSema combination remain under regulatory review. Novo Nordisk is the lead sponsor.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Anticipated as next-generation obesity/T2D treatment with superior weight loss vs semaglutide alone; significant community interest ahead of potential approval
community_insights.top_negative_report: GI side effects (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea) are dose-limiting in a substantial minority during up-titration; injection site reactions reported
community_insights.common_mistake: Self-sourcing from grey-market peptide suppliers who may sell cagrilintide of unverified purity; combining with other GLP-1 agents without medical supervision
community_insights.pro_tip: Slow up-titration over 16-20 weeks substantially reduces GI adverse events; ensure adequate protein intake given significant lean mass loss potential

compounds.name: Cardiogen

administration.frequency: Daily or as directed; Russian protocols typically 10-day courses repeated seasonally

compounds.mechanism_summary: Cardiogen is a Khavinson-developed tetrapeptide (Ala-Glu-Asp-Arg) purported to be a cardiac tissue-specific bioregulator. Proposed mechanism involves epigenetic modulation: the tetrapeptide is postulated to interact with histone-DNA complexes and transcription factor binding sites in cardiomyocytes, upregulating cardioprotective gene expression, enhancing mitochondrial function in cardiac tissue, and supporting myocardial repair. Russian studies claim benefits in cardiac aging, post-infarction recovery, and arrhythmia prevention. The mechanistic data is derived exclusively from Khavinson group publications.

evidence.detail: No Western RCTs or indexed PubMed publications identified for Cardiogen as a specific compound. ClinicalTrials.gov search yielded no registered studies. PubChem CID 16078958 maps to a different compound (a pyrimidine derivative, not the Ala-Glu-Asp-Arg tetrapeptide). ChEMBL search returned no relevant hits. Evidence base consists exclusively of Russian-language publications from the St. Petersburg Institute of Bioregulation and Gerontology, which are uncontrolled and not independently replicated.

regulatory.fda_category: Unapproved peptide; not reviewed by FDA
regulatory.note: Marketed as a dietary supplement in Russia. Not a registered pharmaceutical in Western markets. No regulatory filing on record.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Community use for cardiac health support, anti-aging, and post-cardiovascular-event recovery, primarily among users familiar with Russian bioregulator literature
community_insights.top_negative_report: Complete lack of independent safety verification; sourced from unregulated suppliers with unknown peptide purity
community_insights.common_mistake: Using as a substitute for evidence-based cardioprotective medications; misidentifying PubChem CID as validating the specific tetrapeptide compound
community_insights.pro_tip: If explored, use only alongside, not instead of, established cardiac therapies; verify peptide sequence and purity from any supplier

compounds.name: Cartalax

administration.frequency: Daily or as directed; Russian protocols typically 10-day courses repeated seasonally

compounds.mechanism_summary: Cartalax is a Khavinson-developed tripeptide (Ala-Glu-Asp) proposed to function as a cartilage tissue bioregulator. It is postulated to modulate gene expression in chondrocytes and connective tissue, potentially stimulating collagen synthesis, proteoglycan production, and extracellular matrix maintenance. The tripeptide sequence overlaps with the C-terminus of the Cardiogen tetrapeptide, and Khavinson's group suggests tissue specificity is conferred by the full sequence context and local concentration gradients. Russian studies report benefits in osteoarthritis, joint aging, and cartilage repair.

evidence.detail: No Western RCTs or indexed PubMed publications identified specifically for Cartalax. ClinicalTrials.gov search yielded no registered studies. PubChem CID 16258 maps to 1,3-benzoxazine-2,4-dione, an unrelated heterocyclic compound—not the Ala-Glu-Asp tripeptide. ChEMBL returned no relevant hits. Evidence is confined to Russian-language publications from Khavinson's institute.

regulatory.fda_category: Unapproved peptide; not reviewed by FDA
regulatory.note: Available as a dietary supplement in Russia. Not registered as a pharmaceutical in Western markets.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Community use for joint health support, cartilage regeneration, and anti-aging connective tissue maintenance
community_insights.top_negative_report: No safety data in Western populations; unclear bioavailability of the free tripeptide via oral route
community_insights.common_mistake: Oral bioavailability of short free peptides is inherently uncertain; assuming the tripeptide reaches target tissue intact after digestion
community_insights.pro_tip: Sublingual or subcutaneous routes may improve bioavailability compared to oral; combine with established joint support protocols

compounds.name: Cerebrolysin
compounds.mechanism_summary: See literature: [The effect of neuroprotectors on the level of BDNF, tumor necrosis factor alpha; Cerebrolysin ameliorates ketamine-mediated anxiety and cognitive impairments via

evidence.detail: 8 PubMed results but limited clinical data. Mostly preclinical/mechanistic.

regulatory.note: Hydrated via public APIs. Run MCP hydration for full regulatory detail.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Neuroprotection and cognitive enhancement — approved in 40+ countries for stroke and TBI recovery
community_insights.top_negative_report: Not FDA-approved. Requires IV/IM administration. Derived from porcine brain tissue.
community_insights.common_mistake: Attempting SubQ — cerebrolysin is designed for IM or IV only. Large volumes needed.
community_insights.pro_tip: One of few nootropics with actual clinical trial data (stroke recovery, dementia). Approved widely outside US. Needs medical supervision.

compounds.name: Chonluten

administration.frequency: Daily or as directed; Russian protocols typically 10-day courses

compounds.mechanism_summary: Chonluten is a Khavinson tripeptide (Gly-Glu-Pro) proposed to act as a lung and bronchial tissue bioregulator. The mechanism is hypothesized to involve nuclear penetration, binding to histone-DNA complexes, and activation of cytoprotective and anti-aging gene programs in pulmonary epithelial cells. Overlapping with Bronchogen in tissue targets but structurally distinct (tripeptide vs tetrapeptide), Khavinson publications suggest Chonluten may reduce pulmonary aging markers and support bronchial mucosal integrity. The Gly-Glu-Pro sequence also bears structural similarity to collagen-derived tripeptides, suggesting potential anti-inflammatory collagen mimicry.

evidence.detail: No indexed Western publications or registered clinical trials identified. PubChem CID 91477 corresponds to a cholestanone steroid compound, not the Gly-Glu-Pro tripeptide—indicating the CID is misassigned for this compound in common databases. ChEMBL search returned no relevant hits. Khavinson group publications exist in Russian literature but are not independently replicated.

regulatory.fda_category: Unapproved peptide; not reviewed by FDA
regulatory.note: Marketed as a dietary supplement in Russia. No Western regulatory filing.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Used alongside Bronchogen for lung support; community reports of improved respiratory endurance and recovery after respiratory illness
community_insights.top_negative_report: Overlapping indications with Bronchogen cause confusion; users unsure whether to use both simultaneously
community_insights.common_mistake: Using Chonluten and Bronchogen simultaneously without any evidence base for combined use; dose stacking without understanding interactions
community_insights.pro_tip: Many users alternate between Chonluten and Bronchogen in separate cycles rather than using both concurrently; oral route is common for this tripeptide

compounds.name: Cortagen

administration.frequency: Daily or as directed; Russian protocols typically 10-day courses repeated seasonally

compounds.mechanism_summary: Cortagen is a Khavinson tetrapeptide (Ala-Glu-Asp-Pro) proposed to function as a cerebral cortex bioregulator. Its hypothesized mechanism involves modulation of gene expression in cortical neurons and glial cells via epigenetic mechanisms—binding near promoter regions to upregulate neuroprotective, neuroplasticity, and anti-aging gene programs. Russian publications claim effects on cognitive function, neuroprotection in aging, and recovery from neurological injury. The peptide differs from Cardiogen (Ala-Glu-Asp-Arg) only in the terminal residue, suggesting that tissue specificity in the Khavinson model is determined by the C-terminal amino acid.

evidence.detail: No Western RCTs or indexed PubMed publications identified for Cortagen. ClinicalTrials.gov search yielded no registered studies. PubChem CID 5952 maps to cortisol acetate (deoxycorticosterone acetate analog), an entirely different compound—the CID does not correspond to the Ala-Glu-Asp-Pro tetrapeptide. Evidence is restricted to Khavinson group Russian-language publications.

regulatory.fda_category: Unapproved peptide; not reviewed by FDA
regulatory.note: Available as dietary supplement in Russia. Not registered as a pharmaceutical in Western markets.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Reported by community users as a nootropic and neuroprotective agent; used for cognitive anti-aging stacks alongside other Khavinson peptides
community_insights.top_negative_report: No blood-brain barrier penetration data to confirm CNS delivery of the tetrapeptide; mechanism claims are speculative without Western validation
community_insights.common_mistake: Expecting rapid nootropic effects similar to fast-acting peptides like semax or selank; Cortagen's proposed mechanism is long-term epigenetic rather than acute neurotransmitter modulation
community_insights.pro_tip: Typically used in longer cycles (1-3 months) by community users seeking cumulative neuroprotective rather than acute cognitive effects

compounds.name: Decapeptide-12

administration.frequency: Applied topically 1-2 times daily as part of a skincare regimen

compounds.mechanism_summary: Decapeptide-12 (brand name Lumixyl) is a 10-amino-acid synthetic cosmetic peptide (CAS 137665-91-9, PubChem CID 25087629, MW ~1395 Da, sequence Tyr-Arg-Ser-Arg-Lys-Tyr-Ser-Ser-Trp-Tyr) developed for skin brightening and treatment of hyperpigmentation. Its primary mechanism is competitive inhibition of the tyrosinase enzyme, which catalyzes the rate-limiting step in melanin biosynthesis. By blocking tyrosinase activity, decapeptide-12 reduces melanin production in melanocytes without cytotoxicity. Unlike hydroquinone, it does not induce melanocyte apoptosis, making it suitable for sensitive skin and ethnic skin types. A 2012 clinical study (Kassim et al., J Cosmet Laser Ther) demonstrated efficacy in reducing photodamage-related hyperpigmentation over 24 weeks with good tolerability.

evidence.detail: One published open-label clinical study (PMID 22401652, n=13 completers) demonstrated efficacy for facial hyperpigmentation over 24 weeks. No RCT data identified; study was uncontrolled and small. No ClinicalTrials.gov registered trials found. PubChem CID 25087629 confirmed with synonyms including 'Lumixyl'. ChEMBL search did not return a specific Decapeptide-12 hit. Evidence grade is yellow (observational/open-label human data, no RCT).

regulatory.fda_category: Cosmetic ingredient; not reviewed as a drug by FDA; regulated as a cosmetic under FDCA
regulatory.note: Used in cosmetic formulations (Lumixyl brand). Not classified as a drug. No NDA or 510(k). Available OTC in skincare products in the US and internationally.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Skin brightening for melasma, post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation, and photodamage; preferred by users seeking a gentler alternative to hydroquinone or kojic acid
community_insights.top_negative_report: Slower onset than hydroquinone (weeks to months for visible effect); higher cost relative to conventional brightening agents
community_insights.common_mistake: Using without adequate sunscreen, which negates the brightening effect; expecting results in less than 8 weeks
community_insights.pro_tip: Combine with broad-spectrum SPF 30+ sunscreen and a glycolic acid exfoliant for synergistic efficacy, consistent with the Lumixyl regimen studied clinically

compounds.name: Dermorphin

administration.frequency: Research use only; no established clinical dosing regimen

compounds.mechanism_summary: Dermorphin is a naturally occurring heptapeptide (Tyr-D-Ala-Phe-Gly-Tyr-Pro-Ser-NH2) originally isolated from the skin of the South American frog Phyllomedusa sauvagei (CAS 77614-16-5, PubChem CID 5485199, MW 802.9 Da, CHEMBL278789). It is a highly potent, selective mu-opioid receptor (MOR) agonist. The D-alanine in position 2 confers resistance to enzymatic degradation and dramatically enhances potency—estimated to be 30-40x more potent than morphine at the MOR by weight. Dermorphin binds MOR with high affinity, activating Gi/o-protein signaling to reduce adenylyl cyclase activity, hyperpolarize neurons via GIRK channels, and decrease calcium conductance, resulting in profound analgesia. Its exceptional potency and prolonged duration relative to morphine are well documented in preclinical studies. DALDA, a dermorphin-derived peripherally-restricted analog, has been studied for neuropathic pain.

evidence.detail: Dermorphin itself has not been evaluated in registered human clinical trials (ClinicalTrials.gov returns zero results). ChEMBL CHEMBL278789 confirmed; max phase is null (no clinical development). Preclinical data (animal models) is robust, with multiple PubMed publications documenting mu-opioid pharmacology. Dermorphin-derived analogs (DALDA) have human research data. Banned by USADA and international horse racing authorities due to illicit use as a performance-enhancing doping agent in equine sports. Evidence grade is yellow (strong animal/preclinical data, no human RCTs).

regulatory.fda_category: Schedule I-equivalent research compound in the US; not a scheduled substance per DEA per se but effectively treated as unapproved drug with no legitimate human use pathway
regulatory.note: Banned by USADA, WADA, and most horse racing authorities as a doping substance. No FDA-approved formulation. Research use only in academic settings with appropriate IACUC oversight. Human self-administration is extremely high risk.

community_insights.pro_tip: There is no responsible recreational or biohacking use case; if used in research, have naloxone immediately available and start at microgram-level doses with extreme caution given 30-40x potency versus morphine

compounds.name: DS5
compounds.mechanism_summary: DS5 is a poorly characterized compound with unclear identity in the peptide research community. PubChem CID 81689826 maps to brivaracetam besylate (a bicyclic GABA-related anticonvulsant salt), not a nootropic peptide. The compound referenced in biohacking communities as 'DS5' does not have a consistent chemical identity—it may refer to a research reagent with an internal catalog designation, a Dihexa-related peptide, or a misidentified compound. Dihexa (PNB-0408, N-hexanoic-Tyr-Ile-(6) aminohexanoic amide) is a separate, better-characterized nootropic peptide that acts as an HGF/c-Met pathway potentiator. Without confirmed chemical identity for DS5, mechanism cannot be reliably stated.

evidence.detail: PubMed search for 'DS5 dihexa nootropic' returned zero results. ClinicalTrials.gov search returned no results. PubChem CID 81689826 resolves to brivaracetam besylate (MW 367.5 Da, bicyclic compound with benzenesulfonic acid counterion), not any identified nootropic peptide. ChEMBL and DrugBank searches non-informative. The compound as described in community contexts lacks verifiable identity, published pharmacology, or clinical data.

regulatory.fda_category: Identity uncertain; no FDA review or classification possible
regulatory.note: No regulatory status determinable. The compound lacks a confirmed chemical identity in Western pharmaceutical databases. Brivaracetam (the compound at CID 81689826) is FDA-approved for epilepsy under the brand Briviact, but is unrelated to the nootropic peptide described in community contexts.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Community reports of cognitive enhancement and memory improvement, though these are based on unverified compound identity

compounds.name: Follistatin-315

administration.frequency: Daily subcutaneous injection; community-reported protocol typically 10-30 day cycles

compounds.mechanism_summary: Follistatin-315 is a specific isoform of follistatin (FST), an endogenous glycoprotein that acts primarily as a high-affinity binding and neutralizing protein for myostatin (GDF-8) and activin. PubChem CID 5315 maps to succinylsulfathiazole, an unrelated sulfonamide—indicating the CID is misassigned. Follistatin-315 (FST315) is generated by alternative splicing of the FST gene, producing a 315-amino-acid isoform distinct from FST288 and FST344. FST315 binds myostatin with high affinity, sequestering it and preventing its interaction with the activin receptor IIB (ActRIIB) signaling complex, thereby disinhibiting muscle growth and differentiation pathways (Akt/mTOR, IGF-1, satellite cell activation). Gene therapy studies (NCT01519349, NCT06411366) in Becker muscular dystrophy and frailty demonstrate muscle mass increases. Community use involves recombinant protein administration subcutaneously to inhibit myostatin for muscle hypertrophy.

evidence.detail: ClinicalTrials.gov identified 14 studies involving follistatin including Phase 1 gene therapy trials (NCT01519349 in Becker MD, n=15; NCT06411366 in frailty/aging, n=43) and an early-phase safety/efficacy study for follistatin gene therapy (NCT07285629, recruiting). PubMed confirmed robust preclinical evidence (PMIDs 41460393, 41423190) for follistatin-based myostatin inhibition. ChEMBL search returned no follistatin hits. No registered RCT of subcutaneously administered recombinant follistatin-315 protein in healthy humans identified. Evidence grade is yellow: gene therapy clinical data exists in disease populations, but recombinant protein administration in healthy individuals for muscle enhancement is extrapolated from preclinical and gene therapy data.

regulatory.fda_category: Investigational biologic; recombinant protein form not approved; gene therapy formulations in early-phase clinical trials for specific indications
regulatory.note: Recombinant follistatin-315 protein sold by research peptide suppliers is not FDA-approved. Gene therapy approaches are under IND investigation. Self-administration of research-grade recombinant follistatin carries unknown immunogenicity risks.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Muscle hypertrophy and anti-aging applications; used by advanced bodybuilders and biohackers at ~100 mcg/day; reported significant increases in lean muscle mass in community logs
community_insights.top_negative_report: Potential immunogenicity (antibody formation against exogenous follistatin protein); unknown effects on fertility (follistatin regulates FSH via activin inhibition); theoretical cancer proliferation risk (myostatin/activin inhibition may promote growth in unintended tissues)
community_insights.common_mistake: Underestimating systemic effects beyond skeletal muscle—follistatin also inhibits activin, which affects reproductive function, bone density regulation, and potentially tumor microenvironment
community_insights.pro_tip: Community protocols often use 10-day cycles at 100 mcg/day; monitor FSH/LH if reproductive concerns exist; source only from vendors providing endotoxin testing and mass spectrometry confirmation

compounds.name: Follistatin-344
compounds.mechanism_summary: Follistatin-344 is the most common naturally occurring isoform of follistatin, a glycoprotein that binds and neutralises myostatin (GDF-8) and other TGF-beta superfamily ligands including activin A. By sequestering myostatin, it removes inhibitory signalling on skeletal muscle satellite cells, promoting muscle hypertrophy and limiting atrophy. Differs from FST-315 in possessing a heparin-binding domain (exon 6 region) that prolongs local retention and potentially alters its biodistribution. Phase 1 human gene therapy studies using follistatin gene delivery (AAV vector) in Becker muscular dystrophy and inclusion body myositis have been completed, with modest safety signals. Exogenous peptide administration remains research-only with no human clinical dose established.

evidence.detail: Animal studies demonstrate clear myostatin antagonism and muscle hypertrophy. Human gene therapy trials (NCT01519349, NCT06411366) used follistatin gene delivery rather than exogenous peptide and showed Phase 1 safety signals but no RCTs for the peptide form. Community dose of 100 mcg/day is entirely empirical.

regulatory.note: Not approved for human therapeutic use in any jurisdiction. Gene therapy vector formulations are under clinical investigation. Synthetic or recombinant peptide administration is research-only.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Muscle hypertrophy and anti-catabolic support; stacking with myostatin inhibitors or during PCT
community_insights.top_negative_report: Inconsistent results; difficulty sourcing verified FST-344 versus FST-315; short peptide half-life limits efficacy
community_insights.common_mistake: Confusing FST-344 with FST-315; FST-315 lacks the heparin-binding domain and has different tissue distribution. Overdosing risks systemic activin suppression affecting reproductive hormones.
community_insights.pro_tip: Reconstitute in bacteriostatic water and store at 4°C; SubQ injection at muscle belly may improve local retention due to heparin-binding domain

compounds.name: FOXO4-DRI

administration.frequency: Unknown in humans; mouse studies used intermittent dosing (e.g., 3 consecutive days per cycle)

compounds.mechanism_summary: FOXO4-DRI (D-retro-inverso isoform of FOXO4) is a senolytic peptide that disrupts the FOXO4-p53 protein-protein interaction. Senescent cells aberrantly upregulate FOXO4, which retains p53 in the nucleus and suppresses apoptosis, enabling senescent cell persistence. FOXO4-DRI competitively binds the disordered transactivation domain of p53, releasing it from FOXO4 retention and restoring apoptotic signalling selectively in senescent cells. NMR structural studies (2025, Nature Communications) confirm that both the FOXO4-derived region and the cationic cell-permeability peptide contribute to binding. p53 phosphorylation at Ser15 enhances binding affinity for both FOXO4 and FOXO4-DRI. Applications explored include keloid fibroblast clearance and general senolysis for age-related diseases.

evidence.detail: Robust mouse study data demonstrating selective senescent cell clearance and healthspan extension. Human cell line data published (keloid, 2025). No registered human clinical trials found on ClinicalTrials.gov as of March 2026. Human dose entirely unknown; mouse dose of 5 mg/kg does not translate directly.

regulatory.note: No IND filed or human trials registered. Research-only compound. D-retro-inverso design confers protease resistance, increasing duration of action versus native peptide.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Anti-aging and senolytic protocols; used by longevity-focused experimenters hoping to clear senescent cells
community_insights.top_negative_report: Very high cost per cycle; no confirmed human efficacy; uncertainty around dose translation from mouse
community_insights.common_mistake: Using continuous daily dosing rather than pulse/intermittent protocols as studied in mice; ignoring that the D-amino acid form (DRI) is specifically required for efficacy and protease resistance
community_insights.pro_tip: Mouse studies used 3-day on / rest cycles; community protocols often mirror this. Ensure peptide is genuinely D-retro-inverso form, not L-form, as only DRI is cell-permeable and active

compounds.name: GHK

administration.frequency: Daily to several times per week (topical); 1-2x daily (subcutaneous)

compounds.mechanism_summary: GHK (Gly-His-Lys) is an endogenous human tripeptide (PubChem CID 73587, MW 340.38 Da) that naturally chelates copper(II) to form GHK-Cu. It was originally identified as a liver growth-stimulating factor. Mechanisms include: stimulation of collagen, elastin, and glycosaminoglycan synthesis in fibroblasts; activation of matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-2, MMP-9) balanced with TIMP regulation for ECM remodelling; inhibition of TGF-beta1/Smad2/3 signalling to reduce fibrosis; anti-inflammatory effects via TNF-alpha and IL-6 suppression; antioxidant activity; and upregulation of wound-healing genes. Human skin levels decline with age from ~200 ng/mL (age 20) to ~80 ng/mL (age 60). A Phase 2 RCT (NCT07437586) for topical GHK-Cu gel in acute wound healing is actively recruiting.

evidence.detail: Extensive in vitro and animal data supporting wound healing, anti-fibrotic, and collagen-stimulating effects. Multiple human cosmetic observational studies and one active Phase 2 RCT (NCT07437586, topical GHK-Cu). A completed Phase 4 observational study (NCT05932732) included GHK-Cu as part of Hydrafacial treatment. No published human RCTs specifically for subcutaneous GHK without copper.

regulatory.note: GHK-Cu (copper peptide) is widely used as a cosmetic ingredient and is not FDA-regulated as a drug for topical cosmetic use. Injectable GHK is not approved. INCI name: Tripeptide-1.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Skin rejuvenation, wound healing acceleration, anti-scarring, hair growth stimulation
community_insights.top_negative_report: Copper accumulation concerns with excessive GHK-Cu use; topical formulations often underdosed in commercial products
community_insights.common_mistake: Using GHK without copper and expecting the same effects as GHK-Cu; the copper chelation is integral to many mechanisms
community_insights.pro_tip: Topical GHK-Cu penetrates best in low-pH formulations; subcutaneous GHK (without copper) is used by some for systemic anti-fibrotic effects at 200-500 mcg/day

compounds.name: HGH Fragment 176-191
compounds.mechanism_summary: HGH Fragment 176-191 is a synthetic 16-amino-acid peptide corresponding to the C-terminal region (residues 176-191) of human growth hormone. It retains the lipolytic activity of full-length HGH but lacks the growth-promoting (IGF-1 stimulating) and diabetogenic properties. The fragment activates beta-3 adrenergic receptors on adipocytes and stimulates hormone-sensitive lipase, promoting lipolysis specifically in adipose tissue. Unlike native HGH, it does not stimulate IGF-1 production, does not cause insulin resistance, and has no effect on GH receptor-mediated growth signalling. Originally investigated by Monash University researchers as an obesity treatment under the name AOD-9604.

evidence.detail: Animal studies show potent lipolytic effects without IGF-1 elevation or diabetogenic effects. Human clinical trials for obesity (AOD-9604) were conducted through Phase 3 but failed to demonstrate sufficient weight loss efficacy for FDA approval. No approved human indication. No registered clinical trials found on ClinicalTrials.gov specifically for HGH Fragment 176-191 as a standalone peptide.

regulatory.fda_category: Investigational peptide (failed Phase 3 for obesity as AOD-9604); research only
regulatory.note: AOD-9604 (same peptide) received GRAS status from FDA for use as a food ingredient in 2014, but is not approved as a drug. TGA (Australia) has classified it as a Schedule 4 prescription item. Widely available as a research chemical.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Targeted fat loss without the side effects of full HGH; popular for cutting cycles
community_insights.top_negative_report: Modest real-world fat loss compared to expectations; results are subtle relative to cost
community_insights.common_mistake: Expecting IGF-1-mediated anabolic effects; the fragment does not stimulate IGF-1. Not taking in a fasted state, which reduces lipolytic efficiency
community_insights.pro_tip: Inject 30-60 minutes before fasted cardio or upon waking for maximal lipolytic effect; no need for insulin management unlike full HGH

compounds.name: IGF-1 DES

administration.frequency: Once daily, typically post-exercise at injection site near target muscle

compounds.mechanism_summary: IGF-1 DES (des[1-3]IGF-1) is a truncated form of IGF-1 lacking the N-terminal Gly-Pro-Glu tripeptide. This truncation dramatically reduces its affinity for IGF-binding proteins (IGFBPs) while maintaining near-full affinity for the IGF-1 receptor (IGF-1R). Because IGFBPs normally sequester ~99% of circulating IGF-1, des-IGF-1 is approximately 10-fold more potent than native IGF-1 at stimulating cell proliferation and hypertrophy in vitro and in paracrine contexts. Naturally occurs in the brain and colostrum. The reduced IGFBP binding means it acts locally rather than systemically when injected near target tissue, making it a paracrine-acting growth factor.

evidence.detail: Well-characterised in vitro and animal studies confirming 10-fold greater potency vs native IGF-1 due to IGFBP independence (Ballard et al., 1996). No human clinical trials registered or published for exogenous des-IGF-1 peptide as a performance agent. Clinical opportunities for catabolic states and IBD noted in the literature but not pursued. Human doses entirely community-derived.

regulatory.note: Not approved for human therapeutic use. Available as a research reagent. Community use is unsanctioned.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Local muscle hypertrophy and satellite cell activation when injected intra- or peri-muscularly post-workout
community_insights.top_negative_report: Hypoglycaemia risk, especially with higher doses or without carbohydrate intake post-injection; risk of acromegalic effects with chronic systemic exposure
community_insights.common_mistake: Injecting systemically (venous or distant SubQ) rather than locally near target muscle; this undermines the paracrine advantage and increases systemic exposure
community_insights.pro_tip: Always have fast-acting carbohydrates on hand post-injection; inject immediately post-exercise into the worked muscle for best local effect

compounds.name: IGF-1 LR3

administration.frequency: Once daily or every other day (long half-life allows less frequent dosing)

compounds.mechanism_summary: IGF-1 LR3 (Long R3 IGF-1) is a 83-amino-acid synthetic analogue of IGF-1 with two modifications: an N-terminal 13-amino-acid extension and a Glu3-to-Arg3 substitution. These changes dramatically reduce IGFBP affinity, extending the half-life from approximately 12-15 minutes (native IGF-1) to 20-30 hours. It retains full affinity for the IGF-1 receptor, activating PI3K/Akt and MAPK/ERK pathways to drive muscle protein synthesis, satellite cell activation, glucose uptake, and anti-apoptotic signalling. The extended half-life results in sustained systemic IGF-1R activation, making it a primarily systemic (rather than paracrine) agent.

evidence.detail: No registered human clinical trials found. No peer-reviewed human safety or efficacy data for the LR3 analogue as a performance agent. In vitro and animal research confirms extended half-life and IGFBP independence. Human doses are entirely community-derived with no clinical basis.

regulatory.note: Not approved in any jurisdiction. Sold exclusively as a research reagent. Classified as a prohibited substance in sport (WADA). Note: PubChem CID 0 provided in source data; no valid CID confirmed.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Full-body anabolic and anti-catabolic effects during bulking cycles; longer action window than native IGF-1
community_insights.top_negative_report: Systemic exposure increases hypoglycaemia risk throughout the day; potential for organ and tissue growth beyond muscle (gut hypertrophy, acromegalic features)
community_insights.common_mistake: Underestimating the 20-30 hour half-life and stacking with other IGF-1 pathway activators, leading to prolonged receptor oversaturation
community_insights.pro_tip: Lower doses (20-40 mcg/day) are used by cautious users to reduce systemic side effect burden; cycling 4 weeks on / 4 weeks off is common to prevent receptor downregulation

compounds.name: Livagen
compounds.mechanism_summary: Livagen is a tetrapeptide bioregulator (Lys-Glu-Asp-Ala, KEDA) developed by Russian gerontologist Vladimir Khavinson as a tissue-specific peptide derived from liver. Khavinson peptide bioregulators are proposed to act as 'gene switches' by interacting with chromatin and activating genes silenced during aging in a tissue-specific manner. For Livagen specifically, published Russian research (Kuznik et al., 2020) demonstrates hepatoprotective and immunoprotective effects in animal models of hepatic fibrosis and hepatitis, normalising antioxidant and immune status. Proposed mechanisms include chromatin remodelling (epigenetic activation), enhancement of liver cell regeneration, and restoration of hepatocyte functional markers. Very limited Western peer-reviewed literature exists.

evidence.detail: Data primarily from Russian-language publications and Khavinson institute research. Animal models (hepatitis, fibrosis) show hepato- and immunoprotective efficacy. One English-language review (Kuznik et al., 2020, Advances in Gerontology) supports liver-protective effects. No Western RCTs or clinical trials registered on ClinicalTrials.gov. Evidence is observational/animal-grade.

regulatory.fda_category: Peptide bioregulator / not approved outside Russia; dietary supplement in some markets
regulatory.note: Khavinson peptides including Livagen are commercially available as dietary supplements in Russia (Cytomax/Peptide series). Not approved as drugs in the EU, US, or UK. Western sourcing reliability is poor.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Liver protection and recovery, particularly after hepatotoxic compound cycles; anti-aging stacking protocols
community_insights.top_negative_report: Very limited data and widespread product authenticity concerns outside Russia; most Western products are unverified
community_insights.common_mistake: Expecting dramatic near-term effects; Khavinson bioregulators are proposed to work gradually on gene expression, not acutely
community_insights.pro_tip: Oral bioavailability of short tetrapeptides is debated; sublingual administration or subcutaneous injection is favoured by experienced users to bypass hepatic first-pass

compounds.name: Matrixyl
compounds.mechanism_summary: Matrixyl (Palmitoyl Pentapeptide-4, Pal-KTTKS, PubChem CID 9897237, MW ~802 Da) is a lipopeptide consisting of a palmitoyl fatty acid chain conjugated to the pentapeptide Lys-Thr-Thr-Lys-Ser. The KTTKS sequence is derived from type I procollagen and acts as a matrikine, signalling fibroblasts to increase synthesis of collagen I, collagen III, fibronectin, and hyaluronan. The palmitoyl chain improves skin penetration through the stratum corneum. In vitro and clinical studies demonstrate improved skin firmness, reduction of fine lines and wrinkles, and enhanced dermal matrix density. A 2025 in vitro study (Paccola et al.) confirmed Matrixyl upregulated ECM-related gene expression in human dermal fibroblasts, with synergistic effects when combined with injectable platelet-rich fibrin.

evidence.detail: In vitro data consistently showing collagen I/III and fibronectin upregulation. Several industry-sponsored clinical studies demonstrate reduction in wrinkle depth (split-face, double-blind). Independent RCT data is sparse. No clinical trials registered on ClinicalTrials.gov under Matrixyl or palmitoyl pentapeptide-4. Evidence grade is yellow due to cosmetic-grade clinical evidence without pharmaceutical RCTs.

regulatory.fda_category: Cosmetic ingredient; INCI name Palmitoyl Pentapeptide-4
regulatory.note: Regulated as a cosmetic ingredient in the US, EU, and globally. Not a drug. INCI-listed as Palmitoyl Pentapeptide-4. No drug approval or IND required for topical cosmetic use.
