compounds.name: MGF
compounds.mechanism_summary: MGF (Mechano Growth Factor) refers to the synthetic C-terminal peptide corresponding to the unique E-domain of the IGF-1Eb splice variant, which is upregulated in skeletal muscle in response to mechanical loading and damage. The native IGF-1Eb mRNA is transiently expressed early after muscle injury, correlating with satellite cell activation and myoblast proliferation. The synthetic MGF peptide (typically 24 amino acids from the C-terminal of IGF-1Eb) promotes satellite cell activation and proliferation, and downregulates MyoD, p21, Myogenin, and MEF2A to maintain cells in a proliferative rather than differentiated state. A key distinction: no endogenous MGF peptide corresponding to the synthetic fragment has been isolated from biological tissues or fluids; the peptide's specific in vivo existence remains disputed (Matheny et al., 2010). PEGylated MGF (MGF-PEG) has extended half-life.

evidence.detail: Animal and in vitro studies confirm satellite cell proliferation and anti-differentiation effects (Qin et al., 2012; Matheny et al., 2010). However, whether a discrete MGF peptide product is naturally cleaved from pro-IGF-I in vivo remains unproven. No registered human clinical trials. Human dose entirely community-derived. IGF-1 pathway trials on ClinicalTrials.gov (78 studies found) relate to native IGF-1 or IGF-1/IGFBP-3, not MGF peptide specifically.

regulatory.note: Not approved in any jurisdiction. Classified as prohibited in sport (WADA S2 peptide hormones category). No IND or clinical development programme known.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Post-workout satellite cell activation to enhance muscle recovery and hypertrophy; often stacked with IGF-1 LR3 or used alone in natural athletes
community_insights.top_negative_report: Very short half-life of unmodified MGF (minutes in serum) limits efficacy; PEGylated form (MGF-PEG) is favoured but even less studied
community_insights.common_mistake: Using unmodified MGF believing it survives long enough to act systemically; the peptide is rapidly degraded. Not injecting near the worked muscle to leverage local paracrine effects
community_insights.pro_tip: Inject MGF within 30 minutes post-exercise intra- or peri-muscularly; consider PEGylated MGF for extended action; do not combine with IGF-1 DES on same day due to receptor competition

compounds.name: Nonapeptide-1
compounds.mechanism_summary: Nonapeptide-1 (PubChem CID 10418849, INCI name Nonapeptide-1, also known as Melanostatine 5 or White 05) is a synthetic 9-amino-acid peptide (Met-Pro-D-Phe-Arg-D-Trp-Phe-Lys-Pro-Val-NH2) designed as a competitive antagonist of the alpha-melanocyte-stimulating hormone (alpha-MSH) receptor MC1R. By blocking alpha-MSH binding to MC1R on melanocytes, it inhibits the cAMP-PKA signalling cascade that drives MITF transcription, reducing downstream expression of tyrosinase, TRP-1, and TRP-2, thereby decreasing melanin synthesis. The D-amino acid substitutions at positions 7 and 9 confer protease resistance while maintaining receptor binding. Studies confirm it reduces melanin content and tyrosinase activity in melanocytes, positioning it as a skin-lightening and anti-hyperpigmentation cosmetic active (Chen et al., 2022).

evidence.detail: In vitro data in human melanocytes and keratinocytes confirm MC1R antagonism, reduced melanin content, and tyrosinase inhibition (Chen et al., 2022). Used as a positive control comparator in melanogenesis research. No registered human clinical trials on ClinicalTrials.gov. Cosmetic efficacy data exists but is primarily industry-sponsored.

regulatory.fda_category: Cosmetic ingredient / skin-lightening active; INCI name Nonapeptide-1
regulatory.note: Regulated as a cosmetic ingredient, not a drug. Used in topical depigmentation and brightening formulations. No drug approval required for cosmetic use. Regulatory status varies by country for skin-lightening claims.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Reduction of hyperpigmentation, melasma, and post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation in topical formulations
community_insights.top_negative_report: Effects are modest and slow compared to hydroquinone or kojic acid; topical penetration of the intact nonapeptide is limited
community_insights.common_mistake: Expecting depigmentation equivalent to prescription agents; Nonapeptide-1 is a mild brightening agent best used in combination formulas rather than as a standalone treatment
community_insights.pro_tip: Combine with niacinamide, alpha-arbutin, or ascorbic acid for synergistic melanin inhibition via complementary pathways; use with SPF to prevent re-pigmentation

compounds.name: Ovagen

administration.frequency: Daily cycles typical in Russian protocols (10-day courses)

compounds.mechanism_summary: Ovagen is a Khavinson bioregulator tripeptide with the sequence Glu-Asp-Leu, developed at the St. Petersburg Institute of Bioregulation and Gerontology. Like other Khavinson peptides, it is proposed to act via epigenetic mechanisms: short peptides penetrate cell nuclei, interact with histone proteins and DNA promoter regions, and modulate gene expression in a tissue-specific manner. Ovagen is targeted toward liver and ovarian tissue, theoretically restoring gene transcription patterns associated with these organs. The broader class of Khavinson ultrashort peptides has been shown in vitro and in animal models to interact with nucleosome components and influence methylation status. No Western pharmacological mechanism studies exist for this specific tripeptide.

evidence.detail: No PubMed-indexed English-language publications were returned for Ovagen specifically. No ClinicalTrials.gov registrations found. Evidence is limited to Russian-language Khavinson bioregulator literature and proprietary research from the Institute of Bioregulation and Gerontology. The broader peptide class (Khavinson bioregulators) has animal and some human observational data, but this specific tripeptide lacks independently verifiable Western database coverage.

regulatory.note: Approved and used clinically in Russia as a dietary supplement/cytomedine. Not evaluated by the FDA or EMA. Not scheduled. Sold as a research peptide in Western markets.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Liver support, ovarian function restoration, anti-aging organ-specific bioregulation
community_insights.top_negative_report: Virtually no community dosing experience reported in Western peptide communities; absence of feedback data
community_insights.common_mistake: Conflating Ovagen with other Khavinson peptides (e.g., Epithalamin, Vilon); each has claimed tissue specificity
community_insights.pro_tip: Russian protocols describe 10-day subcutaneous courses repeated 2-3 times per year; oral bioavailability is uncharacterized in Western literature

The proposed mechanism involves binding to and activating neurotrophin receptors, thereby promoting hippocampal neurogenesis, synaptic plasticity, and cognitive enhancement. The peptide name 'P21' appears in some literature in reference to a 21-amino acid peptide or fragment with neurotrophic properties, though the community-circulated compound has limited formal characterization. The compound as described in research peptide communities lacks peer-reviewed pharmacological characterization. compounds.name: Pal-AHK

administration.frequency: As part of topical formulations, applied 1-2 times daily

compounds.mechanism_summary: Palmitoyl-AHK (Pal-AHK) is a cosmetic peptide consisting of the tripeptide Ala-His-Lys conjugated to a palmitoyl (C16) fatty acid chain. The palmitoyl group enhances lipid solubility and skin penetration through the stratum corneum. AHK tripeptide is structurally related to the hair growth peptide domain; it is proposed to stimulate keratinocyte and dermal papilla cell activity, promoting hair follicle cycling and potentially supporting hair density. It may also have ancillary skin-conditioning effects through matrix interactions. The PubChem CID 11683 resolves to a progesterone-related steroid rather than the peptide, confirming a CID mismatch for this compound.

evidence.detail: No dedicated PubMed publications were returned specifically for Pal-AHK. The compound belongs to the palmitoyl peptide class (alongside Pal-GHK, Pal-KTTKS) which has cosmetic in vitro evidence for skin and hair effects. No randomized controlled trials found in ClinicalTrials.gov. Evidence base is primarily in vitro cell culture studies and cosmetic brand white papers. The related class of palmitoyl peptides has moderate cosmetic literature support.

regulatory.fda_category: Cosmetic ingredient (INCI: Palmitoyl Tripeptide-1 related / AHK variant)
regulatory.note: Regulated as a cosmetic ingredient in the US and EU. Not classified as a drug. Listed under cosmetic ingredient databases. Safe for topical use in approved formulations.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Hair growth stimulation, scalp treatment serums, skin firmness in topical cosmetics
community_insights.top_negative_report: Minimal reported adverse effects; occasional mild contact irritation in sensitive individuals
community_insights.common_mistake: Applying in overly high concentrations without proper formulation vehicle, reducing stability and efficacy
community_insights.pro_tip: Effective concentration in cosmetic formulations is typically 1-5 ppm; should be formulated at pH 5-7 to maintain peptide stability

compounds.name: Pal-GHK

administration.frequency: Applied topically 1-2 times daily as part of cosmetic formulation

compounds.mechanism_summary: Palmitoyl-GHK (Pal-GHK) is the palmitoylated derivative of the naturally occurring tripeptide Gly-His-Lys (GHK), conjugated to a 16-carbon palmitoyl chain to improve skin penetration and lipid compatibility. The parent GHK tripeptide is found naturally in human plasma and is a potent copper-chelating sequence with established biological activity: it stimulates collagen, elastin, and glycosaminoglycan synthesis in fibroblasts; promotes wound healing; has anti-inflammatory activity; and supports angiogenesis. Pal-GHK retains these properties while offering improved percutaneous absorption compared to unmodified GHK. The palmitoyl modification increases partition into the lipid-rich stratum corneum. Published research (PMID 39963574) confirms GHK-Cu and Pal-GHK are effective anti-wrinkle peptides with collagen-stimulating properties, though formal clinical data is sparse. The PubChem CID 10231864 matches a palmitoyl-GHK-Lys derivative (Pal-GHK with lysine extension, MW 578.8, C30H54N6O5).

evidence.detail: PubMed returns multiple relevant publications on GHK and Pal-GHK including a 2025 review (PMID 39963574) and structural studies (PMID 23822110). In vitro evidence for collagen stimulation and wound healing is robust. However, controlled clinical trials specifically on Pal-GHK as a standalone intervention are absent from ClinicalTrials.gov. A hydrafacial study (NCT05932732) included GHK-containing products but is not specific to Pal-GHK. Evidence grade is yellow due to strong in vitro and observational data but absence of rigorous RCT data.

regulatory.note: Approved cosmetic ingredient in US and EU. Widely used in anti-aging skin care products. Not classified as a drug. Palmitoyl tripeptide-1 is the INCI name used commercially.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Anti-aging topical use for wrinkle reduction, collagen stimulation, and skin firmness
community_insights.top_negative_report: Rare contact sensitization reported; generally well tolerated
community_insights.common_mistake: Expecting drug-like efficacy from topical application; penetration is limited without enhancement techniques
community_insights.pro_tip: Combining Pal-GHK with penetration enhancers (microneedles, liposomes, or chemical enhancers) significantly improves delivery; works synergistically with retinoids

compounds.name: Palmitoyl Dipeptide-6

administration.frequency: Applied topically 1-2 times daily as part of cosmetic formulation

compounds.mechanism_summary: Palmitoyl Dipeptide-6 is a cosmetic dipeptide (INCI name: Palmitoyl Dipeptide-5 Diaminobutyroyl Hydroxythreonine or similar variants) consisting of a palmitoyl-modified dipeptide designed to stimulate extracellular matrix protein synthesis, particularly collagens type I and IV, fibronectin, and laminin. The palmitoyl chain facilitates membrane interaction and skin penetration. The dipeptide component is proposed to signal dermal fibroblasts to upregulate matrix protein expression, contributing to skin firmness, elasticity, and reduction of wrinkle depth. The PubChem CID 11962376 resolves to a palmitoyl dipeptide (C26H51N3O5, MW 485.7, Pal-Lys-Thr based structure), which is consistent with a cosmetic matrix-targeting dipeptide class.

evidence.detail: No dedicated PubMed publications were returned for Palmitoyl Dipeptide-6 specifically. The compound class (palmitoyl peptides targeting ECM proteins) has in vitro cosmetic evidence. No ClinicalTrials.gov registrations found. Evidence is primarily based on cosmetic ingredient supplier studies and in vitro fibroblast assays. Classified as yellow due to plausible mechanism within a well-characterized peptide class but absence of independent clinical trials.

regulatory.note: Regulated as a cosmetic ingredient in the US and EU. Not classified as a drug. Used in premium anti-aging formulations.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Skin firmness improvement, reduction of deep wrinkles, matrix protein stimulation in anti-aging creams
community_insights.top_negative_report: Generally well tolerated; no significant adverse reports in cosmetic literature
community_insights.common_mistake: Using as a standalone ingredient without appropriate pH buffering; dipeptides require stable formulation pH (5-7)
community_insights.pro_tip: Often combined synergistically with other palmitoyl peptides (Pal-GHK, Pal-KTTKS) in multi-peptide anti-aging formulations for broader matrix stimulation

compounds.name: Pancragen

administration.frequency: 10-day intramuscular courses per Russian protocols; oral capsule forms also used

compounds.mechanism_summary: Pancragen is a Khavinson bioregulator tetrapeptide with the sequence Lys-Glu-Asp-Pro, developed at the St. Petersburg Institute of Bioregulation and Gerontology for pancreatic tissue restoration. Like all Khavinson ultrashort peptides, the proposed mechanism involves penetration into cell nuclei, interaction with chromatin and histone proteins, and epigenetic modulation of gene expression in a tissue-specific manner. Specifically for the pancreas, the peptide is theorized to restore the functional activity of beta cells and normalize insulin/C-peptide secretion. Published animal data (PMID 28509500) demonstrates that Pancragen (0.05 mg/animal/day IM for 10 days) corrected impaired glucose tolerance and normalized insulin and C-peptide levels in old female rhesus monkeys, with effects comparable to glimepiride but with a more physiological normalization profile.

evidence.detail: PubMed returned one directly relevant animal study (PMID 28509500) showing glucose tolerance correction in aged primates, and one related tetrapeptide study (PMID 21246099) examining a Lys-Glu-Asp-Trp variant. Evidence is limited to animal/primate models from a single research group. No ClinicalTrials.gov registrations found. No Western RCT data exists. Classified as yellow rather than red due to published primate data, but evidence remains preclinical and from a single institution.

regulatory.fda_category: Dietary supplement / cytomedine (Russia); research peptide (Western markets)
regulatory.note: Registered and sold as a dietary supplement in Russia. Not evaluated by FDA or EMA. Not scheduled in Western jurisdictions. Sold as a research peptide in international markets.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Pancreatic function support, blood glucose regulation in aging, insulin sensitivity restoration
community_insights.top_negative_report: Very limited Western community experience; hypoglycemia risk theoretically possible given mechanism
community_insights.common_mistake: Using without monitoring blood glucose; the primate data shows real hypoglycemic-direction effects
community_insights.pro_tip: Russian protocols recommend monitoring fasting glucose before and during use; the IM route is used in clinical Russian settings while oral is for maintenance

compounds.name: PE-22-28

administration.frequency: Once daily intranasally; community protocols report 4-day courses (matching original spadin IV studies)

compounds.mechanism_summary: PE-22-28 is a synthetic analogue of spadin, a naturally occurring peptide derived from the propeptide of the neurotensin receptor 3 (NTSR3/Sortilin). Spadin and its analogues, including PE-22-28, act by blocking the TREK-1 two-pore domain potassium channel. TREK-1 (KCNK2) is a mechano-sensitive, temperature-sensitive background K+ channel highly expressed in neurons; its inhibition increases neuronal excitability and serotonergic neurotransmission. In published research on spadin (PMID 20405001), TREK-1 inhibition produced antidepressant effects in mice within 4 days of IV treatment, enhanced hippocampal CREB phosphorylation, promoted neurogenesis, and increased 5-HT neuron firing in the dorsal raphe nucleus. PE-22-28 is a modified analogue designed for improved stability and CNS penetration, with the community using it intranasally to bypass the blood-brain barrier. The CID 9257 resolves to 1H-1,2,4-triazole, not the peptide — CID is a mismatch.

evidence.detail: Strong published animal evidence for the parent molecule spadin (PMID 20405001, PLoS Biology 2010): antidepressant effects in 5 behavioral paradigms, enhanced neurogenesis, CREB activation. PE-22-28 as a specific analogue has limited published human data. No ClinicalTrials.gov registrations found for PE-22-28 or spadin. Evidence is yellow due to robust preclinical mechanism and animal data for the class, with no human RCT evidence.

regulatory.note: Not FDA approved. Not scheduled. No IND filed. Research compound sold in peptide markets. The parent molecule spadin has been studied academically but no therapeutic development has advanced to clinical trials.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Rapid-onset antidepressant effect, mood elevation, neurogenesis support, anxiolytic properties
community_insights.top_negative_report: Some users report mild overstimulation or insomnia; intranasal administration can cause nasal irritation
community_insights.common_mistake: Using daily for extended periods without breaks; original animal data used short 4-day IV courses
community_insights.pro_tip: Community reports suggest effects within 1-3 days of use, consistent with the rapid onset seen in animal studies; cycling 4 days on / 3 days off may mirror the research protocol

compounds.name: PEG-MGF
compounds.mechanism_summary: PEG-MGF (PEGylated Mechano Growth Factor) is a synthetic, PEGylated version of the MGF E-domain peptide, which is a C-terminal splice variant of IGF-1 (IGF-1Ec in humans). Native MGF is produced locally in response to mechanical stimulation or injury in muscle (and other tissues) and acts as a potent activator of satellite cells (muscle stem cells), promoting their proliferation and differentiation for muscle repair and growth. The E-domain peptide (typically a 24-amino acid fragment) signals through a distinct receptor pathway from mature IGF-1 and has been shown to activate satellite cells, suppress IGF-1R internalization, and upregulate local tissue repair mechanisms. PEGylation (polyethylene glycol conjugation) dramatically extends the half-life from minutes (unmodified MGF peptide) to approximately 72-96 hours, enabling systemic distribution and twice-weekly dosing. The PubChem CID 68028837 resolves to a PEG-maleimide linker (MW 257.24), not the full PEG-MGF peptide, confirming a CID mismatch for the large conjugated compound.

evidence.detail: No PubMed results for PEG-MGF specifically. The parent MGF/IGF-1Ec has preclinical literature in muscle biology. ClinicalTrials.gov search for MGF returns 78 studies but these are for IGF-1 broadly, not for MGF E-domain peptide specifically. No trials found for PEG-MGF as a bodybuilding/recovery agent. Evidence is yellow due to solid mechanistic rationale from IGF-1 splice variant biology and animal/cell culture data, but no human clinical trials.

regulatory.note: Not FDA approved. Not scheduled under the Controlled Substances Act but may be prohibited in competitive sports under WADA (World Anti-Doping Agency) regulations as an anabolic agent or growth factor. Sold as a research chemical.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Muscle recovery acceleration, satellite cell activation, injury repair, hypertrophy support alongside training
community_insights.top_negative_report: Localized muscle swelling at injection sites; some report disproportionate site-specific growth if injected intramuscularly
community_insights.common_mistake: Injecting intramuscularly (site-specific growth risk); subcutaneous administration is preferred for more systemic distribution
community_insights.pro_tip: Community protocols suggest administration on non-training days or post-workout for satellite cell activation; stacking with IGF-1 LR3 is common but may be redundant given mechanistic overlap

compounds.name: Pentapeptide-18

administration.frequency: Applied topically 1-2 times daily as part of cosmetic formulation

compounds.mechanism_summary: Pentapeptide-18 (also marketed as Leuphasyl or INCI name Tyr-Arg-Gly-Phe-Leu) is a cosmetic pentapeptide designed to mimic enkephalins (endogenous opioid pentapeptides: Tyr-Gly-Gly-Phe-Met/Leu). Enkephalins interact with delta-opioid receptors in the skin and at the neuromuscular junction, where they can reduce the release of acetylcholine from neuromuscular junctions, partially relaxing facial muscle contractions. This mechanism is sometimes called 'Botox-like' in cosmetic marketing, though the effect is substantially milder than botulinum toxin. Additionally, the peptide may have effects on cutaneous sensory neurons. The PubChem CID 10099522 resolves to a pentapeptide (C29H39N5O7, MW 569.6) with sequence Tyr-Ala-Gly-Phe-Leu (YAGFL), consistent with a Leu-enkephalin analogue structure, confirming peptide identity match.

evidence.detail: No dedicated PubMed publications returned for Pentapeptide-18 or Leuphasyl specifically. No ClinicalTrials.gov registrations found. Evidence is based on cosmetic supplier in vitro and small clinical studies by manufacturers. The enkephalin mimicry mechanism has scientific plausibility based on established opioid receptor pharmacology in skin. Classified as yellow due to mechanistic plausibility and manufacturer-sponsored evidence, but absence of independent peer-reviewed clinical trials.

regulatory.note: Regulated as a cosmetic ingredient in the US and EU. Not classified as a drug despite opioid receptor mimicry mechanism (mechanism at skin level only, not systemic). Safe for topical use.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Expression wrinkle reduction ('Botox-like'), forehead line softening, periocular wrinkle improvement in topical serums
community_insights.top_negative_report: Minimal adverse effects reported; limited evidence of real-world efficacy vs. stronger actives
community_insights.common_mistake: Expecting Botox-equivalent results; the neuromuscular effect is very mild compared to botulinum toxin
community_insights.pro_tip: Often combined with Argireline (Acetyl Hexapeptide-3) for synergistic expression line reduction; the two peptides target different steps in acetylcholine release

compounds.name: PGPIPN

administration.frequency: Not established; research stage compound with no standard dosing protocol

compounds.mechanism_summary: PGPIPN (Pro-Gly-Pro-Ile-Pro-Asn) is a proline-rich hexapeptide. Proline-rich peptides and proline-glycine-proline (PGP) sequences have established roles in extracellular matrix biology and inflammation. PGP tripeptides are well-characterized matrikines derived from collagen degradation, acting as neutrophil chemoattractants via CXCR1/CXCR2 receptors. The extended hexapeptide PGPIPN likely retains some of this proline-glycine-proline signaling scaffold while the additional residues (Ile-Pro-Asn) modify receptor binding or metabolic stability. Proposed uses include wound healing, tissue repair, and anti-inflammatory activity. The PubChem CID 3083038 resolves to a complex phosphonamidate compound (MW 719.8, C40H42N5O6P) with an indole and phthalimide ring system — this is clearly not the hexapeptide PGPIPN, confirming a significant CID mismatch.

evidence.detail: No PubMed results returned for PGPIPN as a hexapeptide. The related PGP tripeptide class (Pro-Gly-Pro) has published literature in inflammatory biology and COPD. No ClinicalTrials.gov registrations found for PGPIPN. No ChEMBL or DrugBank entries identified. As a research-stage compound, formal evidence is absent. Classified as red — no data.

regulatory.note: Not FDA approved. Not scheduled. Research-stage compound with no IND or regulatory filing. Sold in small quantities by peptide research suppliers.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Theoretical wound healing and tissue repair support based on proline-rich peptide biology; anti-inflammatory properties
community_insights.top_negative_report: No community experience data available; essentially no user reports exist in Western peptide forums
community_insights.common_mistake: Purchasing based on extrapolation from PGP tripeptide literature without recognizing the extended hexapeptide has very different pharmacology
community_insights.pro_tip: This compound is truly at the research frontier with no established dosing or safety profile; not recommended for human use outside of formal research settings

compounds.name: Pinealon
compounds.mechanism_summary: Pinealon is a Khavinson bioregulator tripeptide (Glu-Asp-Arg; EDR) derived from pineal gland tissue. It is proposed to act as a gene expression modulator, penetrating cell nuclei and binding to specific DNA sequences to upregulate neuroprotective gene transcription. In Russian preclinical literature, it has been associated with antioxidant effects, regulation of melatonin-related pathways, and suppression of age-related neurodegeneration. The palmitoyl-conjugate form enhances lipid-membrane penetration.

evidence.detail: Evidence is limited to Russian-language preclinical studies and a small number of observational reports by Khavinson et al. No peer-reviewed human RCTs indexed in PubMed were identified. PubChem CID 10273502 confirmed as Glu-Asp-Arg (MW 418.40, formula C15H26N6O8). No ChEMBL or ClinicalTrials.gov entries found.

regulatory.note: Not approved or evaluated by the FDA. Sold as a research chemical in the West. Registered as a dietary supplement/peptide bioregulator in Russia under the Khavinson peptide program.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Reported improvement in sleep quality and vivid dreaming, consistent with proposed pineal/melatonin modulation
community_insights.top_negative_report: Insufficient evidence base; users note little to no subjective effect at standard doses
community_insights.common_mistake: Confusing oral bioavailability with injectable; the peptide is highly hydrophilic (XLogP3 -6.1) and likely has poor oral absorption
community_insights.pro_tip: Some users combine with Epithalon for a broader pineal/aging protocol; subcutaneous injection is preferred for reliable systemic exposure

compounds.name: PNC-27
compounds.mechanism_summary: PNC-27 is a 32-residue chimeric peptide containing the p53 transactivation domain (residues 12-26) fused to a penetratin cell-penetrating peptide leader sequence. It selectively binds to HDM-2 (human homolog of MDM-2) at the plasma membrane of cancer cells, where HDM-2 is aberrantly expressed on the surface. This interaction induces transmembrane pore formation leading to necrotic cancer cell lysis without affecting normal cells. The intact full-length peptide is required for pore formation activity (not degradation fragments).

evidence.detail: Evidence is entirely preclinical (cell lines, in vitro studies). Key publications by Sarafraz-Yazdi, Pincus, Michl et al. (Biomedicines 2022 PMID 35625682; Cancer Chemother Pharmacol 2010 PMID 20182728) confirm mechanism in MCF-7 breast cancer cells. PubChem CID 7474 is a CID mismatch (maps to 1-chloro-4-nitrobenzene, not PNC-27 peptide). No clinical trials registered on ClinicalTrials.gov. No ChEMBL entry for the peptide.

regulatory.note: Strictly a research compound. Not approved for human use by any regulatory agency. No IND or clinical trial registration identified.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Theoretical interest as a selective cancer cell lytic agent with no toxicity to normal cells in preclinical models
community_insights.top_negative_report: No human safety or efficacy data; no route of administration established; peptide stability and delivery remain unsolved challenges
community_insights.common_mistake: Assuming preclinical cell-line selectivity will translate directly to in vivo or human efficacy without further development
community_insights.pro_tip: Monitor Bowne/Pincus/Michl lab publications for any transition to animal model or Phase I trial data

compounds.name: Prostamax
compounds.mechanism_summary: Prostamax is a Khavinson bioregulator tetrapeptide derived from prostate gland tissue, proposed sequence Lys-Glu-Asp-Ala (KEDA) or similar variant. Like other Khavinson peptides, it is proposed to act as an epigenetic gene expression modulator with tissue-specific affinity for prostate epithelial cells. Russian literature claims supportive effects on benign prostatic hyperplasia and age-related prostate function, potentially via anti-inflammatory and anti-proliferative gene regulation.

evidence.detail: PubChem CID 222284 is a CID mismatch (maps to a phytosterol, C29H50O, MW 414.7, not a tetrapeptide). No PubMed results found for Prostamax or Khavinson prostate peptide in English-language literature. No ChEMBL, ClinicalTrials.gov, or DrugBank entries identified. Evidence is limited to Russian-language gray literature.

regulatory.note: Not approved by FDA or EMA. Available as a research chemical and as a Russian registered supplement under the Khavinson bioregulator program (marketed as Prostamax or similar trade names in Russia).

community_insights.top_positive_use: Reported improvements in urinary flow and prostate comfort in older men in Russian cohort data
community_insights.top_negative_report: Virtually no Western database coverage; purity and identity of commercially available products difficult to verify
community_insights.common_mistake: Assuming Russian bioregulator clinical data meets Western evidence standards; most studies lack placebo control and independent replication
community_insights.pro_tip: If sourcing for research, verify peptide identity by HPLC and mass spectrometry given lack of Western regulatory oversight

compounds.name: Rigin

administration.frequency: Once to twice daily (topical); variable subcutaneous

compounds.mechanism_summary: Rigin (Palmitoyl Tetrapeptide-7, Pal-GQPR) is a palmitoylated form of the tetrapeptide Gly-Gln-Pro-Arg (GQPR). It functions as an immune-modulating cosmetic peptide that suppresses production of excess interleukins (particularly IL-6) through a substrate-competitive mechanism. The palmitoyl chain confers lipophilicity enabling skin penetration. It also activates beta-1 integrins, promotes extracellular matrix synthesis, and reduces UV-induced skin inflammation. Systemically, GQPR has immunomodulatory properties relevant to the IgG Fc receptor system.

evidence.detail: PubChem CID 127555 confirmed as Gly-Gln-Pro-Arg (MW 456.5, C18H32N8O6). Published cosmetic efficacy studies demonstrate anti-wrinkle and anti-inflammatory effects in ex vivo skin models and small clinical studies. No large human RCTs for systemic use. ChEMBL search did not return a specific Rigin entry. No ClinicalTrials.gov trials identified for systemic use.

regulatory.note: GQPR is an established cosmetic peptide ingredient (INCI: Palmitoyl Tetrapeptide-7) widely used in anti-aging skincare products. Systemic use as a research peptide is not FDA-approved. Included in the CTFA/INCI cosmetic ingredient database.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Reduction of inflammatory skin redness and improvement of skin firmness when used topically in combination serums
community_insights.top_negative_report: Subcutaneous use for systemic immune modulation lacks safety data and evidence-based dosing
community_insights.common_mistake: Confusing cosmetic topical concentrations (typically 3-5 ppm) with research subcutaneous doses; systemic bioavailability of topical application is negligible
community_insights.pro_tip: Most effective topically when combined with other peptides such as Matrixyl (Palmitoyl Pentapeptide-4) in a liposomal carrier for enhanced penetration

compounds.name: SLU-PP-322
compounds.mechanism_summary: SLU-PP-332 (note: the PubChem CID 5338394 maps to SLU-PP-332, the correct compound; sometimes written SLU-PP-322 in community contexts) is a small-molecule pan-agonist of estrogen-related receptors (ERRalpha, ERRbeta, ERRgamma). ERRs are orphan nuclear receptors that act as master regulators of mitochondrial biogenesis, oxidative metabolism, and exercise-responsive gene transcription. Activation mimics the transcriptional program induced by endurance exercise, increasing mitochondrial content, fatty acid oxidation, and metabolic rate. Chemical formula C18H14N2O2, MW 290.3.

evidence.detail: PubChem CID 5338394 confirmed as 4-hydroxy-N'-[(E)-naphthalen-2-ylmethylidene]benzohydrazide (SLU-PP-332). PubMed 2026 paper (PMID 41850449) documents SAR analysis of the scaffold. No human clinical trials on ClinicalTrials.gov. Research-stage compound only. ChEMBL ID CHEMBL4208749 confirmed with no assigned max_phase. XLogP3 3.7, MW 290.3.

regulatory.note: Research compound only. Not approved or under IND by FDA. No human safety data. Sold as a research chemical by specialty suppliers.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Theoretical interest as a non-exercise approach to improving metabolic fitness, endurance capacity, and fat oxidation based on mouse model data
community_insights.top_negative_report: No human data; unknown safety profile, carcinogenicity concern given ERR involvement in cancer cell metabolism
community_insights.common_mistake: Assuming rodent exercise-mimetic effects will translate to humans at equivalent mg/kg doses without toxicology data
community_insights.pro_tip: Monitor Washington University (Burris lab) publications for progression toward IND-enabling studies before considering any human exposure

compounds.name: SNAP-8

administration.frequency: Topical application once or twice daily

compounds.mechanism_summary: SNAP-8 (Acetyl Octapeptide-3) is a synthetic octapeptide (sequence: Ac-EEMQRRAD-NH2) that mimics the N-terminal fragment of SNAP-25, a component of the SNARE protein complex involved in vesicle fusion and neurotransmitter/neuropeptide release at the neuromuscular junction. By competitive inhibition of the SNARE complex, it reduces the release of catecholamines that stimulate facial muscle contraction, thereby reducing the depth of expression lines. MW approximately 1073 Da (PubChem CID 71587832 confirmed; C42H72N16O15S). It is an extended analogue of Argireline (Acetyl Hexapeptide-3) with potentially greater efficacy.

evidence.detail: PubChem CID 71587832 confirmed (MW 1073.2, C42H72N16O15S). Evidence comes from in vitro SNARE inhibition assays and manufacturer-sponsored clinical studies showing modest reduction of periorbital wrinkle depth (typically 15-25% vs vehicle). No large independent RCTs. No systemic use data. No ClinicalTrials.gov or ChEMBL entries for this specific peptide.

regulatory.note: Used exclusively as a cosmetic ingredient. Not classified as a drug by FDA. INCI name: Acetyl Octapeptide-3. Regulated under cosmetics legislation in the EU and US. Not for injection.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Reduction of forehead and crow's feet wrinkle depth with regular twice-daily topical application; useful botulinum toxin adjunct or alternative for mild cases
community_insights.top_negative_report: Effects are subtle and require consistent use; does not approach botulinum toxin efficacy for deep dynamic lines
community_insights.common_mistake: Using a concentration below 10 ppm in a formulation; effective concentrations in studies are typically 10-50 ppm in an appropriate delivery vehicle
community_insights.pro_tip: Combine with a penetration enhancer (e.g., niacinamide, hyaluronic acid liposomes) and apply to slightly damp skin for improved transdermal delivery

compounds.name: Somatropin
compounds.mechanism_summary: Somatropin is recombinant human growth hormone (rhGH), a 191-amino acid protein identical in sequence to pituitary-derived hGH. It binds the GH receptor (GHR) triggering JAK2-STAT5 signaling, stimulating IGF-1 production primarily in the liver. IGF-1 mediates anabolic effects: increased protein synthesis, lipolysis, muscle hypertrophy, and long bone growth. GH also has direct metabolic effects including insulin antagonism, free fatty acid mobilization, and sodium retention. Clinically approved for GH deficiency (children and adults), Turner syndrome, Prader-Willi syndrome, chronic renal insufficiency, SGA, and HIV-associated wasting.

evidence.detail: FDA-approved (multiple brands: Genotropin, Norditropin, Humatrope, Saizen, Nutropin, Omnitrope, Zomacton). ChEMBL ID CHEMBL1201621, max_phase 4. 807 ClinicalTrials.gov studies found. Extensive RCT evidence for approved indications. Community use at 2-4 IU/day (0.67-1.33 mg/day) for anti-aging/body composition is off-label and not supported by RCTs in healthy adults.

regulatory.note: FDA-approved for multiple indications under various brand names. Prescription required in the US. Use for anti-aging, athletic performance enhancement, or body composition in healthy adults is not FDA-approved and is off-label. DEA Schedule: not a controlled substance, but prescription-only. Distribution for non-approved purposes is subject to FDCA enforcement.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Improved body composition (reduced fat mass, increased lean mass), enhanced recovery, improved skin thickness, and energy at doses of 1-4 IU/day
community_insights.top_negative_report: Carpal tunnel syndrome, water retention/edema, joint pain, and elevated fasting glucose at higher doses (>4 IU/day)
community_insights.common_mistake: Injecting in the morning rather than evening; GH is naturally pulsatile with peak release during slow-wave sleep, and evening dosing better mimics physiological patterns and may reduce glucose intolerance
community_insights.pro_tip: Starting at 1-2 IU/day and titrating based on IGF-1 levels (target upper-normal range for age) minimizes side effects; splitting dose AM/PM at higher doses reduces GH spike magnitude

compounds.name: Syn-Ake

administration.frequency: Topical application once or twice daily

compounds.mechanism_summary: Syn-Ake is a synthetic tripeptide (Diaminobutyroyl Benzylamide Diacetate; INCI: Dipeptide Diaminobutyroyl Benzylamide Diacetate) that mimics the mechanism of Waglerin-1, a peptide from the venom of the Temple Viper (Tropidolaemus wagleri). It acts as a reversible antagonist at the muscular (post-synaptic) nicotinic acetylcholine receptor (nAChR), specifically the epsilon subunit, reducing muscle contraction in facial expression muscles. This reduces dynamic wrinkle formation. PubChem CID 71465152 confirmed as acetic acid salt of the core peptide (C23H37N5O7, MW 495.6).

evidence.detail: PubChem CID 71465152 confirmed (MW 495.6, C23H37N5O7). Evidence from manufacturer-funded studies (DSM/Pentapharm) showing 52% reduction in muscle contraction in in vitro bioassays, and clinical studies reporting ~52% reduction in wrinkle depth after 28 days in small (n~30) sponsored trials. No independent large RCTs. No ClinicalTrials.gov or ChEMBL entries. Topical penetration of this peptide is limited without appropriate delivery vehicles.

regulatory.note: Cosmetic ingredient only. INCI: Dipeptide Diaminobutyroyl Benzylamide Diacetate. Not regulated as a drug in the US or EU at cosmetic concentrations. No injection use; neurotoxicity risk if administered systemically due to nAChR antagonism.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Noticeable softening of expression lines around the eyes and forehead with consistent use; popular in professional serum formulations
community_insights.top_negative_report: Effects are concentration-dependent and formulation-dependent; cheaper products with low Syn-Ake concentration show minimal effect
community_insights.common_mistake: Attempting injection of topical formulations; Syn-Ake is formulated with cosmetic excipients not suitable for injection and has unknown systemic nAChR effects
community_insights.pro_tip: Optimal topical concentration is 4-10% solution (of the commercial 4% active ingredient supply); combine with occlusion or peptide-penetrating carrier technology for maximum efficacy

compounds.name: Syn-Coll

administration.frequency: Topical application once or twice daily

compounds.mechanism_summary: Syn-Coll (Palmitoyl Tripeptide-5; INCI: Palmitoyl Tripeptide-5) is a palmitoylated tripeptide (Pal-Lys-Val-Lys or similar sequence) that activates the TGF-beta (transforming growth factor beta) signaling pathway by mimicking thrombospondin-1 (TSP-1) activation sequences. This stimulates fibroblast production of procollagen Types I, III, and IV, as well as fibronectin and hyaluronic acid, leading to dermal matrix remodeling and improved skin firmness. PubChem CID 11950477 confirmed as Pal-Lys-Val-Lys (C33H65N5O5, MW 611.9). The palmitoyl group confers lipophilicity enabling transdermal penetration.

evidence.detail: PubChem CID 11950477 confirmed (MW 611.9, C33H65N5O5, XLogP3 4.6). Manufacturer and independent cosmetic studies demonstrate increased collagen synthesis in fibroblast cultures and small clinical studies (n=20-40) reporting improved skin elasticity and reduction of wrinkle depth scores over 84-day application periods. No large independent RCTs. No ClinicalTrials.gov or ChEMBL entries.

regulatory.note: Cosmetic ingredient regulated under cosmetic (not drug) frameworks in the US and EU. INCI: Palmitoyl Tripeptide-5. Claims of collagen synthesis stimulation must not be made as drug claims in cosmetic marketing. No systemic or injectable use established.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Long-term improvement in skin firmness and reduction of fine lines with consistent use; often stacked with Matrixyl 3000 or Argireline in anti-aging serums
community_insights.top_negative_report: Slower onset than botulinum toxin alternatives; requires 8-12 weeks of consistent use for visible collagen remodeling effects
community_insights.common_mistake: Using in formulations with pH below 4 or above 8, which can hydrolyze the peptide bond and deactivate the compound
community_insights.pro_tip: Most effective in combination with Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) which provides cofactor support for collagen hydroxylation, and in formulations with pH 5-7 to preserve peptide stability

compounds.name: Tesofensine
compounds.mechanism_summary: Tesofensine (NS-2330) is a presynaptic triple monoamine reuptake inhibitor (TMRI) that blocks reuptake transporters for serotonin (SERT), dopamine (DAT), and norepinephrine (NET). This increases synaptic monoamine concentrations in the hypothalamus and mesolimbic system, producing appetite suppression (via reduced food intake), increased energy expenditure, and reduced fat mass. Structurally a bicyclic tropane derivative (MW 328.3, C17H23Cl2NO; CHEMBL3989690, max_phase 2). It was originally investigated for Alzheimer's disease and Parkinson's disease before being repurposed for obesity.

evidence.detail: Phase 2 RCT data available (NeuroSearch/Saniona). Key Phase 2b trial (Astrup et al., Lancet 2008) showed significant dose-dependent weight loss: 0.5 mg/day achieved ~6.5 kg weight loss vs ~2 kg placebo at 24 weeks. 13 registered ClinicalTrials.gov studies covering obesity, hypothalamic obesity, Type 2 diabetes, and Prader-Willi syndrome. ChEMBL confirmed max_phase 2. Currently developed by Saniona in combination with metoprolol (to mitigate cardiovascular side effects). Never reached Phase 3 or FDA approval.

regulatory.fda_category: Investigational drug (Phase 2); not approved in any jurisdiction
regulatory.note: Not FDA or EMA approved. All human use outside of clinical trials is off-label research use. Obtained as a research chemical. No IND for general use. Saniona continues Phase 2 development as of 2024 for specific obesity subtypes (hypothalamic obesity, Prader-Willi syndrome) with metoprolol co-administration to manage cardiovascular effects.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Strong appetite suppression and significant weight loss (community reports 1-2 kg/week at 0.5 mg/day) with notable energy and mood elevation
community_insights.top_negative_report: Elevated heart rate (tachycardia, +7 bpm at 0.5 mg), dry mouth, nausea, constipation, and insomnia; at higher doses (1 mg) cardiovascular effects become significant
community_insights.common_mistake: Using 1 mg/day doses without cardiovascular monitoring; the 0.5 mg dose has the best efficacy/safety balance from trial data; higher doses increase adverse events disproportionately
community_insights.pro_tip: Some researchers co-administer low-dose beta-1 selective beta-blocker (e.g., metoprolol 25-50 mg) to mitigate tachycardia, consistent with Saniona's clinical development strategy; monitor BP and HR regularly

compounds.name: Testagen

administration.frequency: Daily or every other day for 10-day courses, 2-3 times per year

compounds.mechanism_summary: Testagen (Lys-Glu-Asp-Gly, tetrapeptide) is a Khavinson bioregulator derived from testicular tissue. Its proposed mechanism involves epigenetic regulation of gene expression through interaction with histone proteins and DNA promoter regions, activating tissue-specific genes involved in testicular function and androgen production. Like other Khavinson peptides, it is hypothesized to act as a short signaling peptide that penetrates cell nuclei and modulates chromatin structure, thereby restoring age-related decline in gonadal function. Evidence is exclusively from Russian preclinical and small clinical studies not indexed in Western databases.

evidence.detail: No PubMed-indexed studies, no ClinicalTrials.gov registrations, no ChEMBL or DrugBank entries for Testagen as a peptide. The provided PubChem CID 5995 maps to testosterone propionate, not the tetrapeptide. All evidence derives from Russian-language publications by the Khavinson group, not independently replicated or available in Western peer-reviewed literature.

regulatory.note: Not approved by FDA, EMA, or any Western regulatory agency. Sold as research peptide or supplement. Originates from the Russian Institute of Biogerontology; used in Russian clinical practice under non-Western regulatory frameworks.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Reported use for age-related testosterone decline and testicular function support; anecdotally combined with other Khavinson peptides in longevity stacks
community_insights.top_negative_report: Complete absence of Western clinical data makes risk-benefit assessment impossible; potential for hormonal disruption unknown
community_insights.common_mistake: Assuming equivalence with testosterone therapy; Testagen is not androgenic and does not directly raise testosterone acutely
community_insights.pro_tip: If using, typically cycled 10 days on, several weeks off, consistent with Russian bioregulator protocols; best used alongside Epithalon for comprehensive longevity stacking per Russian gerontology practice

compounds.name: Thymagen

administration.frequency: Daily for 10-day courses, 2-4 times per year

compounds.mechanism_summary: Thymagen (Glu-Trp dipeptide; also known as Oglufanide, Thymogen, Timogen) is a thymus-derived dipeptide immunomodulator. It stimulates T-cell differentiation and maturation, enhances natural killer cell activity, modulates cytokine production (including IL-2, IL-6, interferon), and restores impaired cellular immunity. The compound reached ChEMBL max phase 3 (Oglufanide) for oncology indications including ovarian cancer, where it was studied as IM-862 in combination with chemotherapy. Mechanistically it acts through T-cell receptor signaling pathways and upstream immune activation cascades. ClinicalTrials.gov shows 3 registered trials (NCT00017303, NCT00003773, NCT00006037) for ovarian and colorectal cancer.

evidence.detail: ChEMBL records Oglufanide (Glu-Trp) at max phase 3. ClinicalTrials.gov contains 3 human interventional studies for ovarian cancer (Phase 1 completed, n=43; Phase 2 with chemotherapy; Phase 2 for colorectal cancer terminated due to drug unavailability). PubChem CID 100094 confirmed as Oglufanide/Thymogen (CHEMBL2111029). No large randomized controlled trials demonstrating efficacy. Russian studies on immune restoration exist but are not indexed on PubMed in English. Evidence grade elevated from red to yellow based on Phase 1/2 human trial data.

regulatory.fda_category: Investigational (was studied as IM-862 by Cytran Inc.)
regulatory.note: Investigated as IM-862 (Cytran Inc.) in the US for oncology but development was discontinued. Approved and used clinically in Russia as Thymogen/Timogen nasal spray for immune disorders. Not approved by FDA or EMA. Available as research peptide in Western markets.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Immune restoration after illness, infection prevention, adjunct to cancer treatment support; popular in Khavinson peptide longevity protocols for thymic function
community_insights.top_negative_report: Risk of immune overstimulation in autoimmune-prone individuals; no long-term safety data from Western trials
community_insights.common_mistake: Confusing Thymagen (Glu-Trp dipeptide) with Thymalin (nonapeptide complex) or Thymosin peptides; these are distinct compounds with different mechanisms
community_insights.pro_tip: Intranasal route (used in Russian Thymogen formulations) may offer practical convenience; subcutaneous dosing is used for systemic immune modulation per Russian gerontology protocols

compounds.name: Thymalin

administration.frequency: Daily for 10-day courses (5-20 mg/day), typically 1-2 courses per year

compounds.mechanism_summary: Thymalin (Nonathymulin, Thymulin) is a thymus-derived nonapeptide (pGlu-Ala-Lys-Ser-Gln-Gly-Gly-Ser-Asn) that functions as the primary hormonal signal of thymic origin. It requires zinc for biological activity (zinc-thymulin complex is the active form). Its mechanism involves promoting T-cell differentiation within the thymus, enhancing T-lymphocyte maturation, restoring T-helper/T-suppressor balance, and modulating interleukin production (particularly IL-2 and IL-6). Thymulin levels decline sharply with age due to thymic involution, and supplementation aims to restore youthful immune competence. ChEMBL records it as NONATHYMULIN at max phase 2. PubMed confirms zinc-dependent restoration of neuroendocrine-thymus interaction in aging.

evidence.detail: ChEMBL max phase 2 (CHEMBL2106455, Nonathymulin). PubMed human data exists for thymulin in myasthenia gravis and aging contexts. Russian clinical use documented for gerontological immune restoration. No Western RCTs. ClinicalTrials.gov shows no current registrations for Thymalin.

regulatory.note: Approved and used clinically in Russia (as Timalin) for immunodeficiency, post-surgical recovery, and gerontological indications. Not approved by FDA or EMA. The active nonapeptide is distinct from thymosin alpha-1 (Zadaxin) which has broader international use.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Thymic restoration in aging populations; immune rehabilitation after illness, surgery, or chemotherapy; used in Russian longevity protocols alongside Epithalon
community_insights.top_negative_report: Potential for immune dysregulation with excessive dosing; unknown long-term effects outside Russian clinical context
community_insights.common_mistake: Confusing Thymalin (nonapeptide Nonathymulin) with Thymagen (Glu-Trp dipeptide) or Thymosin Alpha-1; these are distinct peptides with different sequences and regulatory statuses
community_insights.pro_tip: Zinc co-administration may enhance activity since Thymulin is biologically active only as the zinc-thymulin complex; Russian protocols use 5-20 mg/day IM for 10 days

compounds.name: Tripeptide-29

administration.frequency: Topical application as directed by formulation (typically 1-2x daily)

compounds.mechanism_summary: Tripeptide-29 (Gly-Pro-Hyp, glycyl-prolyl-hydroxyproline) is a collagen-derived cosmetic tripeptide that serves as a structural repeat unit of the collagen triple helix. It acts as a collagen precursor signal, stimulating fibroblast production of new collagen (Types I and III) by mimicking breakdown products of extracellular matrix degradation. The peptide also has anti-photoaging activity: a 2024 mouse study (PMID 38411396) demonstrated that Gly-Pro-Hyp is orally bioavailable, accumulates in skin after ingestion of collagen hydrolysates, and reduces UV-induced photoaging by increasing hyaluronic acid, improving elasticity, reducing MMP-1 and MMP-3, and modulating the IL-17/TNF pathway via JUN and FOS transcription factors. Note: PubChem CID 11534242 maps to Biotinoyl Tripeptide-1 (a different cosmetic peptide), not Gly-Pro-Hyp; the provided CID appears to be a mismatch.

evidence.detail: PubMed PMID 38411396 (2024) demonstrates anti-photoaging efficacy of Gly-Pro-Hyp in UV-induced mouse model with mechanistic data including bioavailability, MMP inhibition, and network pharmacology. No human RCTs found. The tripeptide is a well-characterized component of collagen and its role in fibroblast signaling is supported by in vitro cosmetic literature. CID 11534242 mismatch (maps to Biotinoyl Tripeptide-1, not Gly-Pro-Hyp). Evidence is animal/in vitro only.

regulatory.note: Classified as a cosmetic active ingredient under INCI nomenclature. Not regulated as a drug in the US, EU, or other jurisdictions. Topical use only; no pharmaceutical approval. Commonly found in anti-aging serums and creams.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Skin elasticity improvement and fine line reduction when formulated in cosmeceutical serums; often combined with other collagen-stimulating peptides (Matrixyl, Argireline) in premium anti-aging products
community_insights.top_negative_report: Low skin penetration as a free peptide without carrier technology; efficacy highly dependent on formulation quality and delivery system
community_insights.common_mistake: Expecting injectable or systemic effects from topical application; Gly-Pro-Hyp is a topical ingredient only and does not have systemic peptide bioregulator properties
community_insights.pro_tip: Efficacy is enhanced when delivered in nanoparticle or liposomal formulations that improve dermal penetration; look for formulations with penetration enhancers or encapsulation technology

compounds.name: Triptorelin
compounds.mechanism_summary: Gonadotropin-releasing hormone receptor agonist.

evidence.detail: 8 PubMed results but limited clinical data. Mostly preclinical/mechanistic.

regulatory.note: ChEMBL max phase: 0. Hydrated via public APIs. Run MCP hydration for full regulatory detail.

community_insights.top_positive_use: HPTA restart after steroid cycle — single 100mcg dose is common PCT protocol
community_insights.top_negative_report: CONTINUOUS use causes chemical castration (used clinically for prostate cancer). Must be pulsed, not continuous.
community_insights.common_mistake: Using repeated doses — a single 100mcg IM injection is the standard PCT protocol. Repeated dosing SUPPRESSES testosterone.
community_insights.pro_tip: GnRH agonist. A single pulse stimulates LH/FSH. Continuous exposure suppresses them. This is NOT a peptide to cycle.

compounds.name: Vesilute

administration.frequency: Daily for 10-day courses, 2-3 times per year

compounds.mechanism_summary: Vesilute (Lys-Glu-Asp, tripeptide) is a Khavinson bioregulator derived from bladder/urological tissue, proposed to restore function of urogenital epithelium and smooth muscle. The mechanism is hypothesized to follow the general Khavinson epigenetic model: short peptides penetrate cell nuclei, interact with histone proteins and DNA promoter elements, and activate tissue-specific genes involved in bladder epithelial maintenance and smooth muscle function. Proposed benefits include support for age-related urological decline, overactive bladder, and urogenital tissue integrity. Evidence is exclusively from Russian research not indexed in Western biomedical databases.

evidence.detail: No PubMed-indexed studies found for Vesilute. No ClinicalTrials.gov registrations. No ChEMBL or DrugBank entries. PubChem CID 164521 maps to bepotastine besilate (an antihistamine salt), confirming a CID mismatch for the peptide. All evidence is from Russian-origin publications by the Khavinson group, not independently verified in Western literature.

regulatory.note: Not approved by FDA, EMA, or any Western regulatory body. Sold as research peptide. Originates from the Russian Institute of Biogerontology. Used in Russian integrative medicine practice under non-Western frameworks.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Reported use for age-related urological symptoms (frequency, urgency, bladder capacity decline) in older adults; anecdotally combined with other Khavinson peptides in longevity stacks
community_insights.top_negative_report: Complete absence of Western clinical validation; mechanism unverified outside Russian research context
community_insights.common_mistake: Expecting rapid symptomatic relief similar to urological medications; bioregulator peptides are proposed to work via slower epigenetic mechanisms over weeks to months
community_insights.pro_tip: Typically used in 10-day courses consistent with Russian Khavinson protocols; combine with Vesugen (vascular) if urological issues have a vascular component per Russian clinical practice

compounds.name: Vesugen

administration.frequency: Daily for 10-day courses, 2-3 times per year

compounds.mechanism_summary: Vesugen is a Khavinson bioregulator tripeptide (Lys-Glu-Asp variant, also reported as Lys-Glu-Asp-Gly or a related sequence) derived from vascular tissue, proposed to support vascular endothelial and smooth muscle cell function. The mechanism follows the Khavinson epigenetic model: the tripeptide interacts with histone proteins and chromatin, activating genes involved in endothelial integrity, vascular tone regulation, and angiogenesis. Proposed benefits include support for age-related vascular deterioration, endothelial dysfunction, and cardiovascular health maintenance. Published Khavinson group research suggests effects on vascular cell gene expression. Note: PubChem CID 3672 maps to ibuprofen (racemic form), confirming a CID mismatch for this peptide.

evidence.detail: No PubMed-indexed studies found for Vesugen specifically. No ClinicalTrials.gov registrations. PubChem CID 3672 is ibuprofen (CID mismatch confirmed). ChEMBL and DrugBank return no entries for Vesugen. The Khavinson group's general peptide epigenetic mechanism is supported by review literature (PMID 34834147, 35457077) but Vesugen-specific evidence is absent from Western databases.

regulatory.note: Not approved by FDA, EMA, or any Western regulatory body. Sold as research peptide. Part of the Russian Khavinson bioregulator series for cardiovascular and vascular health. Used in Russian integrative and gerontological medicine.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Reported use for vascular aging, endothelial health support, and cardiovascular longevity in older adults; commonly stacked with Epithalon in Russian longevity protocols
community_insights.top_negative_report: No Western clinical evidence; risk profile unknown; some users report mild hypotensive-like effects (unverified)
community_insights.common_mistake: Treating Vesugen as an acute cardiovascular treatment equivalent to established drugs; it is proposed to work on a slow epigenetic timescale, not as an acute vasoactive agent
community_insights.pro_tip: Used in 10-day courses per Russian Khavinson protocols; often combined with Cartalax (cartilage) or Libidon (prostate) in comprehensive aging support regimens

compounds.name: Vialox

administration.frequency: Topical application 1-2x daily as directed by formulation

compounds.mechanism_summary: Vialox (Pentapeptide-3, Gly-Pro-Arg-Pro-Ala-NH2) is a cosmetic pentapeptide that acts as a nicotinic acetylcholine receptor (nAChR) antagonist at the neuromuscular junction of facial muscles. By competing with acetylcholine at post-synaptic nAChR, it reduces muscle contraction signal transmission in a reversible, non-toxic manner. This relaxes the repetitive facial muscle contractions responsible for expression lines and wrinkles, producing a Botox-like effect through a topical route. PubChem CID 11605666 confirmed as Vialox/Pentapeptide-3 (SMILES and synonyms verified). Unlike neurotoxin-based approaches, it acts through competitive receptor antagonism without causing permanent denervation. ChEMBL ID not specifically identified for this compound in the top search results.

evidence.detail: PubChem CID 11605666 confirmed as Pentapeptide-3/Vialox. Mechanism of nAChR antagonism is well-documented in cosmetic peptide literature. No PubMed-indexed controlled human clinical trials found. Manufacturer in vitro and proprietary clinical studies claim 49% reduction in wrinkle depth, but these are not published in peer-reviewed journals accessible via PubMed. Evidence grade yellow based on plausible mechanistic data and manufacturer studies, but absence of independent peer-reviewed clinical confirmation.

regulatory.note: Classified as a cosmetic active ingredient under INCI nomenclature. Not regulated as a drug. Topical use only. Sometimes listed as Pentapeptide-18 or Vialox in cosmetic ingredient lists. No pharmaceutical approval in any jurisdiction.

community_insights.top_positive_use: Topical wrinkle relaxation in eye-area and forehead serums; marketed as a needle-free Botox alternative; popular in luxury anti-aging skincare formulations
community_insights.top_negative_report: Limited skin penetration of intact peptides; effects subtle and temporary compared to injectable neuromodulators; consumer expectations often exceed what topical delivery can achieve
community_insights.common_mistake: Expecting Botox-equivalent wrinkle elimination from topical application; peptide skin penetration is fundamentally limited and concentration reaching the neuromuscular junction is a fraction of applied dose
community_insights.pro_tip: Best results reported when formulated with penetration enhancers or in a galvanic-delivery system; effectiveness is site-specific and most pronounced in areas with repetitive fine expression lines rather than deep static wrinkles

compounds.name: Vilon

administration.frequency: Daily for 10-day courses, 2-4 times per year

compounds.mechanism_summary: Vilon (Lys-Glu dipeptide, H-Lys-Glu-OH) is a Khavinson bioregulator dipeptide with immunomodulatory and proposed longevity effects. PubChem CID 7010502 confirmed (synonyms: Peptide vilon, AB-0 peptide, Normophthal, lysylglutamic acid, CHEMBL365790). SMILES confirmed as the Lys-Glu dipeptide. Mechanistically, Vilon activates T-cell and natural killer cell function, restores immune homeostasis, and has been investigated for effects on telomerase activity and telomere length in aging cell populations. The Khavinson epigenetic model proposes that Vilon interacts with histone proteins and DNA regulatory elements to activate genes governing immune function and cell longevity. Some Russian human trial data exists for immune restoration in aging populations. General Khavinson peptide epigenetic mechanisms are supported by Western review literature (PMIDs 34834147, 35457077).

evidence.detail: PubChem CID 7010502 confirmed as Vilon/Lys-Glu (Peptide vilon synonym confirmed). CHEMBL365790 entry exists. Some Russian human trial data reported for immune modulation in aging adults, but not indexed in PubMed or ClinicalTrials.gov. No Western RCTs. The Khavinson group has published English-language reviews on peptide epigenetic mechanisms (PMID 34834147: Molecules 2021; PMID 35457077: IJMS 2022) that provide mechanistic context. ClinicalTrials.gov returned no results for Vilon. Evidence grade elevated to yellow based on some human trial data from Russian literature and a plausible, published mechanistic framework, despite absence of Western RCTs.

regulatory.note: Not approved by FDA, EMA, or any Western regulatory agency. Used in Russian gerontological and immunological clinical practice. Sold as research peptide in Western markets. Also known as Normophthal (ophthalmic use in Russia for age-related eye conditions).

community_insights.top_positive_use: Immune system support in aging; longevity stacking with other Khavinson peptides (particularly Epithalon); reported use for immune restoration after immunosuppressive illness or treatment
community_insights.top_negative_report: Lack of Western clinical evidence; some users report no perceptible effects; difficulty distinguishing from placebo without biomarker monitoring
community_insights.common_mistake: Expecting rapid subjective effects; Vilon and similar Khavinson dipeptides are proposed to work via slow epigenetic mechanisms with cumulative effects over months of cyclic use
community_insights.pro_tip: In Russian gerontological protocols, Vilon is typically used in 10-day courses 2-4 times per year alongside Epithalon; monitoring immune markers (CBC with differential, NK cell activity) is the most practical way to assess response

community_insights.top_positive_use: Immune modulation and CIRS (chronic inflammatory response syndrome) treatment — popularized by Dr. Shoemaker protocol
community_insights.top_negative_report: Must be used as final step in Shoemaker protocol. Can worsen symptoms if used prematurely. Limited evidence.
community_insights.common_mistake: Using VIP before completing upstream CIRS treatments (binders, antifungals, etc.)
community_insights.pro_tip: Vasoactive intestinal peptide — naturally produced. Intranasal is standard route for CIRS. Must be compounded fresh.
